So, like I say, there's I I kind of view it as slightly selfish that I'm trying to I want to build this company that way way outlives me. Um, and can go on and have, you know, long long impact. Is there any decisions that you make with that kind of understanding of this like multi-deade you're in this basically until the day that you die understanding in the back of your mind um that you might not make if you weren't planning on trying to create this super enduring institution almost. >> Um well I mean if you look at there's there's a lot of space companies out there where if their founders and leaders die I think you'd have to question whether or not that company would continue. >> I think Elon's one of them. I like I think the like keeper of the soul of those companies is is Elon and the second that he leaves. >> Yeah. Well, I mean there's there's there's there's there's more than more than that as well. I mean there's others there as well. And I just didn't want to be one of those companies like that. What a what I mean I I I want this thing to go on for forever. So um so you know that that's kind of you know a critical a critical point and um I'm also a realist like um you know there'll be a point in time where um you know there's other people that can run this company better than I can or I just get too old and I'm I'm you know I'm like that guy trying to log into his laptop and not been able to do it cuz it's so you know you know everybody that comes to this company knows that you have a finite period of time at Rocket Lab and that finite period of time might be that you just get exhausted and you want to do something else or there's other younger better people that are that are better than you and you'll be replaced. And the same goes for me like there'll be a point in time where there's a better person to run this company and and you know I want to make sure that that when that happens this thing doesn't stop. If you have a whole bunch of cash in your bank account, it's pretty easy to graph, you know, how long do we have as far as runway goes? Yeah. >> But when do you want to be like a profitable business so that you just are like operating off of, you know, you're able to make these big bets and you're completely like with the understanding that your other bets that you've made have succeeded and they're able to fund them. >> Yep. Well, it's a complex question and the answer is is as soon as possible because that ensures survival. Um, if you're making money, you will live. If you're not making money, eventually you will die. So it's survival. Um now um you have to you have to play that carefully because okay um you know Rocket Lab could be profitable instantly if we just stop developing Neutron. But Neutron unlocks an entire you know massive opportunity with us building our own constellation and ultimately deploying our own infrastructure in orbit. It's all about risk versus reward. So, you know, do you want to have a little company that's profitable and okay, may survive or do you want to have a much bigger impact and make some investments in these much bigger capabilities? Um, so we're very black and white and impassionate about these sorts of things. Um, for us, um, you know, we don't we we we we don't we don't make decisions based on, you know, feelings and and those kinds of things. It's it's very much uh impassionate um you know we're trying to build profitable we're trying to build value which ultimately builds longevity but um you know we do also you know happy to make uh you know make the decisions and take the risk to to pay to make big investments like neutron where we can see the outcome of that investment is just going to be like orders of magnitude of scale of company. One of the things that Elon did is he just kept on working on basically this idea of launch cost up to orbit or like mass orbit and then getting the cost down and then eventually he basically just filled his own supply uh where now like Starlink is the best business in space period end of story. How do you kind of think about figuring out which business to build in space when you're just absorbing all of your own supply? >> Yep. Well, for the longest time, I've always said it's going to be really blurry in the future of what is a space company and what is not a space company. Especially the big ones like the the the big space companies of the future, they're going to be blurry. And I think if you looked at looked at SpaceX right now, what is it? Is it an AI company? Is it a communications company? Or is it a space company? Um, but the one thing is sure as [ __ ] If if if you have the ability to to put infrastructure in orbit and you have the ability to make that infrastructure that it is in orbit, then your ability to deliver a service or a a solution down on Earth is vastly superior to anybody else. That experiment has been run. That experiment experiment to us is always super obvious. And and I think, you know, that that is clearly where where we're tracking. And um you know I think if we have this in another interview in 10 years time there's going to be a select few of really large space companies that um provide a critical part of infrastructure and backbone in orbit and 100% of them are going to have their own ride to their ride their own rocket. 100% of them. If >> you had to fast forward 5 or 10 years out and you said if you know just best guess today what the vast majority of your launches like what payload they're carrying what would that be like? What is that business that you create? >> Yeah. Yeah, we don't talk about that. >> Oh, >> no. Look, I mean, [clears throat] we don't talk about it for a number of reasons. One, one, uh, it's just too early. Um, so, you know, there's no I'm I'm I'm a very, um, methodical person. So, I like to finish one thing before I start the next. And, uh, the most important thing is to get Neutron to orbit um, and get that vehicle scaled. Uh, then we have the capability to deploy whatever infrastructure we need. Um, you know, we've been methodically uh building capability in our space systems division to the point now whether you want to go to Mars, whether you want to go to the moon, whether you want to do national security, the most exquisite thereof, we can do it all like piece of cake. There is nothing we cannot build now in in orbit. So, um, so you know, we've we've put all those building blocks in place. >> Basically, building optionality >> 100%. The only thing that we've got left to do is to get a neutron finished. Then then we then we can talk about what infrastructure we'll end up building in space. >> In 2012, uh Mark Zuckerberg and Paul Graham were talking together on stage and I think Facebook had just gone public and their stock was down like 50%. Or something like this >> and Paul was asking him basically the question of do you like it when everything is working or do you like to be underestimated? And Mark basically said I love being underestimated and people not really knowing what I'm capable of. What is your favorite state? Is it being underestimated? I like everything working 100%. No, screw that. Yeah. No, I don't I'm uh I don't need to be felt as the underdog or anything like that. No, I like everything humming and working. Absolutely. >> What is the uh scaleup of Neutron look like? >> Yeah. So, uh, we we'll do a first launch and we'll we'll do a splashdown of that and that'll that'll give us a good really good sense of, you know, where we're at with the program. And then, uh, all going well, if the program's going well, then we'll slip the barge under the next one and we'll we'll try and stick a landing. And then, you know, Neutron will scale a lot faster than even Electron scaled. um because we've just put in place uh you know uh both from a design perspective but also just from a a manufacturing and infrastructure perspective we're just so far further ahead than we were with with the likes of Electron I mean you know the design design decisions that we made you carbon composite structure using AFP uh automotive fiber placement machine like it's just nuts how quick we can make a tank or make structures with that with that machine um it's just yeah and how cheap we can do it so um so you know, we've we've chosen various kind of, you know, paths that that are just infinitely scalable. >> I remember Starship initially it was going to be this like carbon fiber basically structure and then they swapped it to like a special alloy of steel. >> Yeah. Are you doing anything new on like the material side? >> Yeah, absolutely. Yeah. I mean um so it's a it's a complicated um so a rocket is a giant engineering compromise. Um like if we're if we're having a design review and all the all the subsystems are in a room together and one of them is happy then something's gone wrong. Like everybody should be equally as miserable because it is everything is a giant engineering compromise. And um you know uh carbon is you know strength to weight ratio is just vastly superior to any other material. Like it's four times better than stainless steel for example. You know for the same strength it's it's it's one quarter of the weight. So um but with that comes other challenges and you know we've been able to solve these challenges through a lot of this automation with AFP machines. So you know the structures of neutron are just absurdly light um uh which is you know which enables you to uh you know take pressure off other areas like um rocket engines. Our rocket engines don't have to be like on the limit of grenades. um they can they can be they can be backed right off and just sort of you know a relatively unstressed engine and that's because you know we've have our structures are so light that you know we can back some performance off the propulsion. >> What's been the biggest unlock over the past like 20 years that you wouldn't have expected like most unexpected thing that have happened? Well, um there's lots of things. Uh there's both from a technology standpoint and also I would say um capital because is a big one because we always had in this position where we have vastly less resources than our competitors and um you know even as a public company that that is that is true. But as a public company, we've been able to harness the, you know, the capital access that we have and just look how fast this business has grown like yearon year. It's just crazy. Not just organically, but inorganically we're able to acquire capabilities and and grow those as well. So, you know, the the the kind of, you know, the line here is fairly vertical with respect to that. So, you know, going public um was a great capital unlocker for us to to go and and you know, really really grow. I mean, we we went public with one product and now we have hundreds and we're building, you know, complete as a prime contractor for the US government on national security. So, a lot of that was unlocked by, you know, having that having that ability to access that capital. >> I've gotten this overwhelming sense of just relentless optimism from you and from just the team in general. >> What is the importance of having just extreme optimism? Well, I mean, I think I think um yeah, I mean, it's it's it's it's absolutely critical, but I think what a boring person if you're always down. I mean, like, would you want to work with somebody who has just like took the negative side of the angle? I mean, >> never. But the but the thing is most people aren't like you just constantly have a smile on your face every time I see you. [laughter] >> Yeah. Well, I mean, I'm having fun. So, it's not it's not it's not um it's not anything other than that, but um No, I think I think there's a lot to be optimistic about. like this is we're doing fun stuff and we're doing big stuff that's important. Um so I don't not sure how you could be anything but an optimist um in this in this industry. And I and it it probably to be fair it probably does attract optimists because um you know the rocket gods are trying to crush you every every chance they get. So you have to you know you have to be a little bit a little bit sunny side up.

3CEinhANrdg.title: Manufacturing 1 million drones a year | Soren Monroe-Anderson, Neros
3CEinhANrdg.text: On first pass, we've defeated every single jammer [music] that the US military has put us up against. So, we played a bit of a prank on the soldiers. They knew our frequencies we were operating on. We'd already done a bunch of testing runs and they collected data. We flew 7 km over the jammer and then just hovered in front of them for like 10 minutes. After 10 minutes with five jammers going at once, they got to taking out one of our control links. We have two. [music] We asked them on the radio like, "Hey, can you guys see the payload?" And they're like, "Yep, we see you have a payload." And we're like, "No, can you see what the payload is?" We strapped a jar of Smucker's strawberry jam to our drone as the payload and then the soldier saw the Smucker's jam. The jammer has been defeated by the jam. In a real world scenario, we would just have come in and hit the target. Today I have the pleasure of sitting down with Saurin Monroe Anderson, the co-founder and CEO of Nuros. Nuros is, I think today, the biggest FPV drone maker in America. When I interviewed you last, I think you were just moving into your old warehouse which has like a max capacity of 10, 20, 30,000 drones a year and we are now in your new 250,000t warehouse with a max capacity of a million drones per year. Do you want to just start with like what was the ramp like at that old warehouse and what are you going to be able to do here? >> Yeah, so we moved into our old facility in March of 2024. We had been in a very small garage before that. We had built like a couple hundred drones out of that little garage, but we knew ramping production capacity was kind of the number one goal of the company at that point. Um, and we wanted to be able to we didn't have the orders for lots of drones, but we wanted to be able to say like, look, we can do it. It took us about 6 months after moving into that facility to really have like a real uh assembly line going that we could kind of credibly say like, "Give us the order, we'll start delivering." Um, but it wasn't until early 2025 that we were really um like shipping more than a few hundred drones per month. So, it's honestly been a really fast ramp up over the last 12 months and also just to fill that facility, not just from the production side, but the team size. uh you know we were nearest was 16 people when 2025 started and uh we are currently about 125 and I'd guess that number is going to be even higher just by the you know time this interview comes out we're we're growing the team very rapidly >> almost 10x a year >> um yeah at at this rate yeah um so you know we thought we only had like a I think three-ear [snorts] lease on the old building so It's not like we were planning to be in there for a really long time, but we did uh you know, we really pushed the old facility to its limits now with um both production and engineering. And so when we were we started looking for a new building actually um almost a year ago now because we were very picky on certain things. So, we wanted to start the process early and we went through a diff a few different phases of like what size of building we were going to get and you know we ended up landing on this 250,000 because we over the past 12 months have really oriented the company around being the first American drone company that can build a million drones per year. So we wanted to spec a facility that could support that goal and not just assembly and test but component manufacturing. We've been with our current production running into limitations on what we can get from suppliers outside of China and we sort of known for a long time that to do what we want to do eventually it will really involve vertical integration. But with the demand signal we're seeing right now from the US government and from our allied partners and just the honestly just the direction that the the world is going and how important FPV systems are. Um building massive sovereign capacity is really our number one mandate. So we wanted to find a size facility that could support the long-term growth of the company, not be another, you know, 2, threeear stepping stone, be something that we can actually build into the global headquarters of Nuros. Um, you know, it will be a lot of construction even to just get the like office space that we need in here. Uh, and then, you know, the an empty building doesn't equal a million drones per year. uh there's huge capex investments and and build out and just uh great, you know, engineering that needs to be done to to actually turn this into a factory. So, it's, you know, we we haven't done the thing, but we've we've bought oursel a canvas to do the thing. >> You basically have all the the paints and the canvas ready to go, and now you just got to go paint something. >> Yeah. Um I'd say the the paint is still, you know, some of that's still lacking with with talent that we need to hire. Um, you know, we have an amazing team and we have the right building blocks, but we got to get a lot more great people into to be able to do to do this. >> Very few people have kind of had a pulse like you have on how the Russia Ukraine conflict has kind of evolved. So, do you want to just talk about what it was like going into early 2022 and what's happened over the past three and a half years? >> Yeah, coming up on I mean basically four years now. Uh yeah, you know, Nurus has not been around for for all of that. Uh but I've I've been closely tracking since the full scale invasion started and when it started, it was a very impactful moment for me. And I've talked about this in the past, so I won't go totally deep into that, but um I it's been very very interesting to see how it's made the world react to the technology that Nerus is building because you know we sort of had our own ideas even before the we saw the Ukrainians doing it uh and before NerS was a company we had our own ideas of drone racing technology applied to defense applications and you know thought it could be meaningful but couldn't prove it really. Um, and then the Ukrainians went and proved it. And then in 2023 when we were starting the company, we were just focused on building a better product for the Ukrainians that we were working with. And the the excitement from the US side was really limited. There was like most most people in the Pentagon did not care about small quadcopters. Um, kind of understandably because they had not been a meaningful capability increase uh for the most part. So it w from from what they had seen um focusing on Ukraine for the first you know 12 months of the company was um I think the right decision because we ended up making a much more useful product out of that and it's still a really big focus of the company. Um but it's been interesting to see that the you know the mindset in America has totally flipped 180. Um, small drones are now a top priority uh from, you know, the senior leaders of of the Pentagon and of the country. So, we definitely started at the right time and built up a >> like a competency incredibly fast. >> Exactly. We built up a competency and then we were ready to deliver for these initial programs that the US is spinning up. Um, and now we are trying to build capacity ahead of the government actually putting out contracts for, you know, millions of drones per year. There there's been some talk from the Pentagon and from like the Army saying that or they're they're going to try to acquire a million drones over the next year or two. Um, nobody exists to to deliver that right now. Uh, and I think, you know, that's not going to happen on a on a one or twoear timeline. I think two to three years we're going to start to be able to deliver that and the country is going to start to be able to deliver that. Um and we're going to push to go as fast as possible. But it's uh you know the government is is trying to provide some amount of demand signal. Industry is is responding. Um I would say we are out ahead in terms of FPV drone manufacturers. Um just in terms of our actual like proven production scale. Um, and now with, you know, with this facility, again, it's just a big empty box right now. Uh, but it will be the largest, uh, the largest small drone factory in America. Um, and yeah, it's going to it's going to allow us to the way we think about it is it's going to allow us to create credible deterrence. I think under sort of under this scale and without having the blueprint for factories like this, America does not have credible deterrence. Uh, and so that's what we were trying to build to put it most simply. >> If there's not a blueprint that exists on how to spin up some sort of supply chain and factory like you're trying to do here, how are you kind of coming up with that? You know, from first principles, there are things to look at outside of drones. So, um, I've given this example a few times, but uh, of course, the SpaceX um, Starlink user terminal factory is a really great example. I mean the best example of modern consumer electronics manufacturing in America. Incredibly high rate. um actually a lot of similarities between the products uh in terms of you know the like core processes that make up the the product and um you know being a very uh electronics and RF heavy system and and then you have sort of this more simple structure um that your your main electronics go into and um basically you know the way you build Starlink user terminals and the way you build drones are not that far away from each other um and SpaceX has been able to do that at the you know millions per year Um, and the full assembled product in that vertically integrated factory is cheaper than what they could do in China, which is unbelievable. Uh, and that's that's the bar. So, um, >> that's like a first for America, right? >> It's a first for America in the last 10 years. Uh, you know, we used to own a lot more manufacturing like that. And there's still some companies, you know, really big CMS that operate in America, but now they also, you know, they operate in a ton of countries and most of them have pretty strong ties into China now. And it was like America taught China how to do all the stuff that they're doing. Then China went and realized the advantage that and and made a concerted effort to be better at it um and to scale it way beyond anybody else. Um, so the the thing like the limitations you know we run into is is like electronics in America are so expensive and the lead time is much longer and the quality is much lower. Uh, and and I believe SpaceX is the largest PCB manufacturer in America now. Um, it's not one of the, you know, large board houses u because and there aren't that many. It's it's SpaceX, I'm pretty sure. Um, so that's sort of the blueprint. Um, but a lot of what we need to do is look at China and understand how their drone factories are operating. Um, a lot of the stuff is not wildly complex on its own, but building an entire factory that operates efficiently and then produces a high performance system. That's where the that's where the the magic is. It's it's the like tying the you know performance of the product and every single little design detail that goes into that back into something that is manufacturable and then iterating you know being able to iterate quickly while producing at high volume. Um so that's why it is such an advantage to own the factory and own the product is like there there's this iteration loop that you do not get without that and you need to have engineers who are responsible for you know their part being both performant and manufacturable and understanding the impacts to the customer when they change something but also understanding the impacts to the line when they change something. So this is why uh at SpaceX when an engineer is uh designing a part they're owning everything through all the way through manufacturing. And that's the same mindset that we take. Um it's it's you know not just like the responsible engineering mindset but having people who own the entire life cycle of the of the part design >> and for the evolution of your own technology over the past you know couple years. What have been the biggest step function improvements uh in capability? >> So, we've spent a lot of time on radios and making jamming resistant radios. So, it's one of the number one things that you deal with in FPV drones uh on the battlefield is uh is electronic warfare. So, we've gone through a ton of different iterations on that. Um, we've made our the efficiency of our drone uh really second to none. Um, like every size category that we have a drone in, uh, I've never seen a more efficient drone. I've also, >> how do you do that? >> Uh, we're really good at flight performance. Um, and we, I think, test in a very unique way. Um, we're super analytical in our testing. We care a lot about the performance of the system. So there's a there's a lot of drone companies and FPV companies that sort of either care about building components or care about building drones. They don't a lot of them don't really like build the most performant components to build the most performant drones. Um we do and we we uh try to make a very high performance integrated product um because we own basically all the component design as well. Um, and then there are, you know, we've heard very, very frequently from the actual end users that they really appreciate the way our drone flies, the amount of precision it gives them, um, and just how well it's built. Um, we've had units in Ukraine tell us that this is the first actual like military spec drone they've ever used. Uh, and they've used, you know, all the FPVs. Um, so that's pretty cool. Um, and and that's been a a good bit of work to get there. Um, and in the past, you know, we've definitely shipped products that are not up to like the spec that I want for a for a military system. Um, and even on the current product, there's tons of stuff that needs to be improved and we're working on we're working on new revisions of of everything and a lot of new products right now. So, over the next few months, there'll be a lot of really cool stuff coming out um, and being visible publicly. that's going to be pretty significant um changes in the way our our system can operate. So, I'm really excited about those as well. Um I can't talk about most of the the biggest step changes that we've had because a lot of those have been recent and not announced yet. Um, but I I'll say that our the sort of cohesiveness and performance of our entire system across the ground station to the drone and the way the operator interfaces with all that, um, it's going to get a a really big level up soon. >> You're scaling up production, but you also have to basically be improving the product as fast as possible. What's going through your mind when you know something can be improved, but you also have to ship at the same time. So, you got to make that trade-off. Yeah, it's honestly it can be such a frustrating feeling to even see, you know, we we have products that are awesome. We have like prototypes of them that we are flying with internally, like we're using every single day, but they're not production products. There's a huge difference between a prototype and a production product. And so there's this like you have to it's a little frustrating to be like looking at this thing that's so beautiful that you want all of your customers to have, but it's like 6 months away even though it already exists. um because the time it takes to actually properly qualify something and move it into production, do your production validation builds, uh and then actually start shipping it to the customer. There's there's a decent lead time there. Um, and yeah, so it's it's like we we also try to measure how much we can show the new stuff to current customers cuz they get really excited and they want to buy it too quickly and we're [laughter] like, hold on, you know, this isn't a production product. Like if we if we try to ship this to you, it will have a lot of problems. It will be, you know, not very efficient for us because we're doing these like small batches. So we kind of limit the exposure of the new stuff until it's really ready. Uh, but it's always a feeling of I wish this was, you know, I wish we could ship it right now. >> Are you just basically like living, I don't know, 6 months or 12 months in the future and then you're having to ship the 12 month, you know, 6 12-month old product simply because of that? >> Most of the time, yes. Yeah. Um, but this is I mean this is quite normal. Uh I think it's just like learning learning it um as we build up the sort of suite of products in the company and and improve our the way we design products and the way we validate products and move them over to production. So there's a lot of like learning here but you know many many companies deal with this. Um it's just kind of funny to to see it. >> One of the things that you're most focused on is you want to be like the first drone that is just fully made in America uh with all the components and like subasssemblies and everything. um how close are you to that and like what are the key constraints to actually getting there? >> I mean if it's just about how far down the supply chain you go um there there's just every level is is hard work. Um >> do you want to talk about each of those levels? Yeah, I mean, you know, we we've gone so the the biggest supply chain hurdle we've had to clear so far is getting all of the quote critical components China free. Um, and then we've gone beyond that uh definition as well. So we, you know, we we go down to things like motors as well. Um, and then also we've gone down to the neodymium that goes into the magnets that goes into our motors. Uh, but that's not work that we can always do directly. I mean, most of it's not, right? It's actually just finding the right supplier and building a partnership with them, building a relationship with them. Um, and so when I say it's like just how far down the supply chain you go, you know, Nuros is not going to be the company that can solve like neodymium uh mining and refining in America. At least not for a long time. Um, >> not going to go full Tesla where you're like building the lithium plant. >> Not yet. Um, we can't, you know, we we have to build uh we have to build high rate assembly and test. then we have to build high rate um component manufacturing then you know certain subco components but you you start to get to like you know chips and magnets and those things are obviously insanely hard um you don't you don't get to do those until you're you just hugely scaled so a lot of this stuff is a is a team effort for for America and for allied countries um to go back to your original question you we're quite far away from America, from a drone that is fully made in America because if you if you want to do that, you're talking about every microchip, every uh, you know, mineral. It's just like it's such an impossible list. Um, I actually don't think made in America is you know, fully made in America is the most important stat though. It's it's um can we build drones with an allied supply chain um with certain like biases towards America or other regions depending on who the customer is? Um but like you know could the allied world build a number of drones that is competitive with China um and you know the countries like Russia? Uh that's what we care about. uh more than anything but then for for each customer there is a a bias towards having you know very domestic supply chains and manufacturing which is is understandable. So over time we'll figure out how to do that. One of the things that you talked about with me right before we started was this idea of there's part of it which is you want to be able to have you know there's building a million drones a year and then there's also the cap capability to build a million drones and right now we just don't have the capability. So how are you thinking about basically making sure as quickly as possible we have that capability even if you're not necessarily producing a million. That's why we're focusing really hard on the factory. Um, and and also thinking about if we are building a factory that's capable of peaking at a million drones per year, what rate can it run at that is sustainable um, which is much lower than a million per year. And also, you know, what what are the effective ways of of financing and selling capacity versus just drones. So those are some of the questions that that exist um for for that right like the we can't control the demand side and there is no program for a million drones per year yet and so we have to think about what is the most existential thing which is the capacity um and then and then build around that and we're very very willing to invest our own capital into that vision. well before the government is is paying for a million drones >> just like you did on the first factory. >> Yeah, but the you know the capex investment in the first factory is not uh that significant. The really like the team and the engineering costs are and the inventory the working capital costs have been the biggest um have been the biggest factors so far. the investment into component manufacturing um and automated assembly and test at millions per year scale like that that's where the um that's where it becomes a serious financial question but we're willing to take a good amount of that risk ourselves uh because we're fundamentally a missiondriven company we know this thing needs to exist so it would be somewhat sill silly in my opinion to wait around for uh for the government to to say, "Okay, yeah, we're going to buy all those drones." Um, you have to operate on some level of uncertainty. >> Yeah, this is a little bit more like uh the SpaceX situation with just figuring out launch cost to orbit and like mass orbit and how do we just scale that as quickly as possible? And it turns out, you know, at least in their case, uh the best customer was just themselves for that mass orbit, right? Um and if you just listen to the government, you wouldn't scale it. >> Yeah, absolutely. Um and and you know the government doesn't need to be our only customer for uh forever. It it shouldn't be actually. Um but we have to start if we're trying to fix the drone industrial base, we have to start in defense because right now or you know in defense is where the regulatory environment and the customer exists. That that just totally excludes the Chinese competitors. uh because it's not possible to go head-to-head with the Chinese competitors in the um you know consumer enterprise market right now. Uh because at the end of the day, you know, the human warfighter uh is is like the thing that we value and it's the the the it's the decision maker. Um and and uh again, the the the precision control element is really important. Um so we we've worked a lot on that. Um, as we move forward, we're thinking about how do we give leverage to that person? How do we make it so they can effectively control multiple systems at once? How can they for the targets where you don't need to manually pilot it into the exact spot um just say, you know, click click here, go there. Um, that is all stuff that we're we're working on. So I think the future is going to look like each war fighter able to do more and the barrier for entry being much lower. Um so part of the problem with FPV systems is that they're quite hard to use. Um there's a lot of nuance in using them. So you compare like a DJI drone versus an FPV drone. Basically anyone can fly a DJI drone out of the box and it just works. You don't have to do anything to it. And then you move over to an FPV system. It's like >> you need a highly skilled pilot. >> Yeah. Like if I told you to go charge my FPV drone battery, you would not do it correctly. Like the settings on the chart, you know, this is such a specific, but it's like that's the level of detail and nuance that exists in the systems right now that should go away. So it will become much easier over time um if we do our job correctly for these systems to be widely distributed um and and you know easy for every war fighter to to take advantage of.

HbPxncZvWk8.title: Building Unmanned Cargo Planes | David Zagaynov, Poseidon
HbPxncZvWk8.text: Today I'm interviewing David Zaganov and he is the co-founder and CEO of Poseidon. Uh do you want to just talk about the cargo market in general and how you got into it? >> Yeah, so before Poseidon I was working at Amazon and Amazon ships like tens of millions of packages every single day at a scale like impossible to like comprehend really. Um, I I can go on my phone right now and I can get my groceries delivered by like 400 p.m. I can get a box in at like by 8:00 a.m. tomorrow morning if I pay like $2 extra. And so I think there's been a massive uh the world's gotten like incredibly small where we can buy something from Europe, from China, from the other side of the United States and it gets to us immediately. Um, and that unlocks a bunch of super interesting stuff uh, in terms of like supply chains, logistics. We're able to build stuff in San Francisco, in LA, wherever. And it's like one of one of the most complicated >> like challenges think of. Yeah. Like logistics are a logistical challenge, which is >> fascinating, I think. >> What are you inspired by on the logistics side? Like why does that kind of like light a fire you? >> I think logistics are like the backbone of like today's economy. Um, they're the backbone of any like military operation. Um, they're just incredibly important to like everyday life. I think if our logistics network like stopped functioning tomorrow, uh, life would look incredibly different, we wouldn't be able to like get these like nice Red Bulls in our office every week. >> What is the like first thing that's going through your mind on how to even break into that? because I know with, you know, Amazon, prior to Amazon, the only reason that that was startable was because USPS already existed and there was a whole bunch of roads paved and all this stuff. Um, how are you kind of thinking about it from like a perspective 2025? Uh looking forward >> when when I was working at Amazon, one of the things I like noticed was that like we had hyper optimized like a bunch of our logistics infrastructure in terms of like these distribution centers and like the vans that they get moved on like actually like getting things from like point A to point B. But like one of the like large things that we we hadn't optimized are the the planes that we're flying everything on. And so these planes are like largely the same that like had gotten developed and certified in the 70s and 80s which is like absolutely insane to me that we hadn't made any big leaps forward there. And so Parker and I started looking around at like what can be done in this space. So I know that Poseidon basically started out as a like ground effect company and you were trying to build like a plane that took advantage of that and now you're kind of shifting towards cargo. How did Poseidon start? What was that initial idea and then why are you kind of shifting towards this now? Yeah, I think when Poseidon started, we had seen uh like the Soviet ground effect vehicles and chronoplons as they called them in the 60s and 70s which were these massive massive like like plain boat hybrids that flew very close to the surface of the water and took advantage of ground effect uh which gave them increased lift decreased drag um and we saw that as a technology that we wanted to like bring forth into the 21st century. Um I think in in the meanwhile like the the mission has stayed the same where it's we want to reduce cost per flight ton mile and this was a way that we could do it. Um and so ground effect vehicles are great in like certain operational environments where you don't have to fly over land and your waves are like such a height that like you can be taking advantage of this. Um, but I think what we realized pretty quickly was we didn't want to be just constrained to like a certain market in a certain time. Uh, and we wanted to solve cost per flight 10 mile across the entire air cargo spectrum. And so ground effect vehicles are still great. We're still building seplanes, but we're just expanding beyond that as well. >> I know you mentioned that basically planes have there's been very little innovation, you know, over the last like 50 plus years. Um, why is that the case and like what are you guys going to be able to do differently now? >> Yeah. So, it's a combo of a few factors. Uh, the first one is like when these vehicles got certified initially. Um, there's a bunch of upfront costs that goes with that. >> Is that like hundreds of millions of dollars per vehicle? >> Yes. Yeah. Up to billions of dollars per vehicles for like the 747s and the like the narrow body wide bodies. Um, and so that upfront cost is very prohibitive from doing like very large step changes. You can't just like send a new patch note out like you can with software. And so then what happens is like iterative improvements where they'll have the the frame that's been certified, the engine that's been certified and they'll make small improvements to that and along at the same time the airlines buy the planes and those are large up for cost and so they want to just like maintain and run them for as long as possible. And so between those two there's been no like real push for like innovation on that front. Um, so the average like regional air cargo line is flying at planes that are [snorts] an average of 35 to 40 years old. And so these these are old old airframes and like haven't haven't been updated to like modern tech. >> I know that the way that like cars work or have historically is you sell the car almost like like Ford would sell a truck almost at a loss and then they just make up the margin on like the parts as they replace. Yeah, that's very much the case especially with like engines um where there's the the initial cost of the engine, but like the rebuilds and the maintenance is where a lot of the the OEMs make their money >> with the knowledge that it costs, you know, hundreds of millions or like billions of dollars to kind of do like a groundup rebuild and like certification for a new like vehicle. Um how are you guys going about that? Yeah. So, one of the reasons that it is so expensive to get like build a new vehicle and certify it is because there are people on board. And so, like I think one of the realizations for us was like if we want to optimize like cost per flight ton mile, like the thing to do is take the pilot out. And if you take the pilot out, you get rid of the cockpit and the windows and all the life support systems and like a ton of weight and space that you weren't using for anything effective. And then you could your like airframe can be built differently. You're not beholden to the same standards. Yeah. So, so like because of that like because you weigh less, your engines can be smaller, they can be cheaper, and so all around there's like cost savings in getting rid of that pilot. And it also helps massively with like the certification process. >> Yeah. Does it go down? Is is most of the certification process basically making sure that the human is not going to die in the plane? >> The certification process exists to make sure that like these vehicles are being like safely operated. Uh, and so ideally when there's people on board, you want to protect the lives of the people, but you also really care about like the people that you're flying over um and like those populated areas. And so the like the FAA does its job great when they're like ensuring that our skies are safe. When you're kind of thinking forward and you're saying you want to minimize the number of days and between now and when you're actually like shipping cargo. Uh, how are you thinking about making that plan if it's going to take like I imagine like over a year, multiple years maybe to actually have the first plane in the air delivering stuff. >> Yeah, we're actually planning on having our first plane in the air uh mid next year. So, we're in a full-on sprint right now to build both Heron and Egret. Heron being our SE plane platform, Ingret being our land plane platform and getting the first test flights next summer. Um, and the the the sprint towards getting commercial operations after that is getting certification as quickly as possible. And so we'll first be able to fly in areas that have like low population. Um, >> we we have areas where we'll be able to like test immediately and then as we get like flight hours, uh, we'll work like towards certification. There's a pretty clear path that way. >> You said that you're like sprinting towards that. How are you kind of spending your time and what are you focusing on >> right now? We are in the middle of manufacturing of the planes and so is >> that massive shell that I saw. >> Yeah. Yeah. What does what does that look like? It's like building the wing, building the fuselage, doing carbon fiber layup. And like I think like what one of the interesting things about like the way that we do our manufacturing process is like we are incredibly vertically integrated and we do most of it inhouse. Composits are like absolute magic. Um and like the they've really like matured as technology over the past like decade and they're becoming more and more prevalent in uh aerospace. Um what's cool is like composits are super light um super strong in a marine environment. They don't have corrosion which is like a huge problem that like sea planes experience. >> It's like the same situation as you know the salt uh water where like Ulyses has to design their vehicle so that it's not going to get damaged by sea water. Same situation. >> Yeah. like saltwater corrosion is like a big problem if you put like any metal in the water. Edges are like historically what sea planes experience. And then also you could do like the like mathematically optimal design for your hole for your wings uh because of the way that you make molds uh and you just like lay the carbon fiber into it and so you can preserve like the the perfect like optimal shape. Um, and [snorts] the like manufacturing process for us ends up being like a lot closer to that of like composite boat manufacturing than exquisite like aerospace manufacturing. Like we're we're not building like F-35s or F-22s where these like take like years and like like hundreds of people to thousands of people like across like dozens of states making like parts like we're able to like do all of it under one roof. Um and like minimize our part count significantly by like going like composite carbon fiber. >> I think the 747 or Boeing at least has like 120,000 different suppliers. How many like parts are you able to kind of eliminate just by doing it with a with a model where it doesn't have a human and you're kind of like trying to cast or not not cast but basically form the entire shell in one go. >> It's about like a magnitude reduction in parts which is pretty nuts. >> Let's go through the like iteration process on how you just built uh the first the first few iterations and then I think this was like the first guy that really flew, right? >> Yeah. Yeah. So we built um off the riv after we started the company we built like a first prototype. This was like a initial like ground effect vehicle seplane design with a box wing design that like was basically optimized to fly very close to the water. What we quickly realized that like flying outside of like a very small envelope uh you lose most of your controllability. >> And one time we we were like trying to figure out where we can actually test this. Um and so we went out to uh the lake at Golden Gate Park at 1:00 a.m. because that's when there would be no one there. And this is like an RC test boat lake that's like been approved for like testing like little RC boats. Um, and so so we put our plane in the water and we have the the guys from Starship, Sean and Hugo were there with us and we do a couple test flights. We like take off. We're like flying very close to the water and we try to go like up higher and lose control and like thing flips and it starts drowning immediately and I I just like strip completely like run into the water like swim like 100 meters and like drag it back. like this is the nastiest lake ever. There's like like the the floor of the lake was like a foot of like duck poop. Um I I thought I was going to catch some disease, but yeah, like shortly after then we went back to the drawing board and we're like, "Okay, here are like the design parameters. Here's what we want to be able to do. Like let's just redesign from the ground up." Um and that's what like Seagull is. And so seagull took us about 6 months from like start of design to our first test flight which I think was like a very quick turnaround with three and a half engineers. Um I'm halficient. Yeah. So yeah that that was cool. And then yeah we we did our first test flight um like March. >> What was the goal for that first test flight? What were you trying to achieve? >> We were trying to achieve flight. Um [laughter] like uh yeah. So so like get getting up and off of the water. uh was like entire goal goal of the first test flight and then subsequent test flights we validated like our payload and our range um and like our autonomous controls and like what like sat link looked like and like today uh seagull still serves as a test bed platform for our avionics and our controls algorithms and so even as we're in the middle of like building our first prototypes of heron and egret we're able to test a lot of our uh designs on seagull Interesting. So, you're able to kind of like test all the uh the parts uh that you're going to probably put onto the next, you know, platform without actually having the platform ready. >> Yeah. A lot of the software side of what we're building out can get tested on on Seagull. >> Are you glad that you did that before you tried to go straight into the cargo market? >> So much. I think like even from the get- go like we knew that Seagull was a quarter scale prototype. Um, but I think that there were a ton of learnings across the board. Like even like in so far as like how do we do a carbon fiber layup? Like how do we actually run an aircraft program? Like from clean sheet design to manufacturing and there's a lot of things that we did right and a lot of things that like we learned how to do better. Um, but yeah. No, I I think it would be really challenging for us to just go like right from the rip to to two 50 foot planes. >> Yeah. Yeah. Tell me about uh what went wrong. >> Um I think like the design for manufacturability um is like a lesson that like is incredibly important because there are like techniques that are feasible to do for like a one-off prototype that like would be a massive pain in the butt to do like when we're like pumping out like hundreds of planes. And so that's been a really important learning. Um I learned that I am not like the the world's best avionics engineer. Um, like thank god our team has now expanded to have people who like are incredibly excellent at what they do. >> Do you have any other um really good stories of like extreme scrainess like you know going out to the lake at 1 a.m.? >> Yeah. Uh our uh our our test pilot is this guy Rick who we found on a forum. >> And so we we were looking for someone to like test fly our plane uh for the first time. And so the the the very first test flight was like with like RC controls. >> So we wanted to make sure that like all of our control services were like functioning as properly like the aerodynamics, the hydrodnamics were working. And so we found this guy who ran like North California's like largest RC test like club. Um and they just like they love flying like RC planes. And so he like flies people's like massive like RC prototypes uh for the first time. And so we like called him over like, "Hey, like do do you want to like fly our sea plane?" He was like, "That is the largest like sea plane drone I've ever seen." But like, "Yeah, no, I'd love to do it." And so like we met up with him. We handed them the controls like just like trust that he was not going to like crash. And he he did excellent. >> He did excellent. >> Yeah. >> Yeah. Let's say that everything goes right and next year uh your first version of the actual cargo plane is flying around. How are you going to go from that to actually like shipping real cargo in the plane? >> Yeah. So, um I think there there's a bit like of a crawl run on both like manufacturing and like test and development. And so when we test fly our vehicles for the first time, we are going to uh have a bunch of learnings about like how to like optimize their flight. Um like continue to iterate our like avionics and controls. Um and then as as we're like talking to customers and our partners, uh we want to be able to like deliver to them as as quickly as possible. And so then it's like we're we have 25,000 square feet in San Francisco and it's a good amount of space to to build like the first ones barely um but not to stand up like full manufacturing. And so we're we're going to like quickly build our first like factory where we'll do like initial rate production and then uh after that it's like a large factory to to be pumping out like tens and dozens of these uh like on a monthly basis. in like a slightly different world. If you were teleported back and you had to work at uh on some like plane program from anywhere from like 20th century, what would that be? I think that the planes like immediately after like the Wright brothers planes uh were like terribly interesting because this was a moment like we we knew flight was achievable. We knew that like if we have an air foil and we like produce some thrust so we can get up off the air, like not much else was known about like how do we like build like great planes. Um, and I think that like wartime is a uh like a forcing function for development to like be accelerated. Uh, and there's like very specific like things that we need to optimize for. And so like the first planes that were flying in World War I would have been incredibly interesting to be working on. I'd imagine that you you were able to get uh something approved and actually in the air much easier during war. >> Yeah. I don't even know what their approvals looked like. I think it was like, "All right, who wants who wants to pilot this thing and be like, "All right, cool. Like, I'll go." Um, yeah, like at at the beginning at the beginning of the conflict, like beginning of World War I, there weren't really that many like like fighter planes flying. But by the end, like they like had quickly ramped up manufacturing production or like tons of pilots like up in the skies. >> Yeah. Do you know how that happened? Like I'd imagine that you'd probably want to take learnings from like the initial like high ramp uh maybe even during World War I and two where you just like started throwing like thousands and thousands of planes into the air uh from basically zero prior to that. >> Um and I imagine that you'd want to kind of like copy that sort of ramp if you're able to uh with Poseidon. Yeah, I I think there's like like specifically like industrial capacity during like World War II is like a super interesting example to look at where um going into that we didn't have like industrial capacity in the way that we needed. Um, and so like factories that like built cars and like like home appliances like quickly like took the the like the same like labor and workforce um and applied it to like building like planes and turning out like bombers and fighter jets and that was like actually like it it was like a huge lift but they were able to achieve in like very little time. Um, but I think there's like a clear roadmap to like scaling production and the way that we're like designing and building our planes. It's like like they're very much designed for being able to like build many of these as as cheap of a price as possible. >> We're building like the the cargo truck for the skies. It's like the like good cheap reliable like F-150 uh for like moving things around in the sky. >> How does a typical week look like for you right now? Um I think like time time split between like like building engineering um and like like developing like partnerships businesses like we we're like um like that is like dual use and so we have uh our like defense federal market as well as like a commercial market and what's great about that is like we our vehicles like are like our our platforms are like applicable between uh like both markets. Um but like each of those have like very different go to market strategies and like very different challenges they have to face. >> Yeah. Do you is the uh like defense market a little bit easier to get something? I'd imagine that their approval process is a little bit simpler than the commercial market. >> Uh I like I don't know if I would say that like called like pre like defense procurement easy by any means. Um but there >> it's a skill issue. >> Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. Um, but there are like advantages and disadvantages to like operating in each market. Like being dual use is great because you can get like the best of both worlds in a way that like it's very hard to sell something commercially that doesn't exist and isn't ready to like commercially operate. Um, but the DO does a lot of funding of R&D and so they will like fund programs that aren't at like TRL 9. Um, so like the TRL scale is like from 1 to nine of like how how ready is this and like six being like a prototype and then like seven being like tested and like as you mature towards nine that's like a product that's ready to be like commercialized. Um and so we're able to get initial traction uh and testing >> on that side and then you can go to the commercial side. uh and like having the commercial business line uh helps bridge like what is like the the value of death where there's uh in in between the the like resources that you can get for like initial like R&D and testing and like actual programs and like being implemented at the end of the day is like a decent chunk of like time and resources and companies that are just like purely defense are often going to struggle with like how do you like bridge that gap and you just need like resources And so having um a like a consistently growing uh like business line that's the commercial helps with that. >> I'd imagine this is a little bit similar to kind of like a drone startup.
