It's, you know, you your performance is the worst of your 10,000 suppliers, you know, and and so it's it's pretty harrowing and uh and the scale if the company's growing fast and you're building a big ambitious product, the scale and scope of what you're doing means that like a couple months of not producing is probably like bankruptcy for the company. And so we went through that moment with platform 2. You know, we knew that platform 2 was serving a market that was 100 to a thousand times the size of the market that Zipline had originally focused on, which was just healthcare logistics in rural areas. Uh you know platform 2 which you know is operating behind us is designed to really enable teleportation like logistics from any business health system from a hospital healthcare facility warehouse retail store or restaurant directly to homes in a way that's 10 times as fast and dramatically less expensive. But um you know platform 2 was a way more complicated product and the scope and scale of what we were trying to do was uh was more ambitious and we wound up being you know 6 months late shipping the product and it felt like the companies I mean it was certainly the case that I think had we gone another six months I think Zeppelin would not have survived and so it was like we just got into this hardcore mode where it was like there was no op there were no other options like we knew what we had to do >> the boats >> yeah the burn the boats were burned And uh there was no no one was going to give us more money and so it was like we needed to launch this product and this was only this was only about a year and a half ago um or even less than that like a year ago um and the team worked through Christmas and everybody canceled their vacations between Christmas and New Year's and uh and then we launched the product on January 15th. How have you kind of thought about the hardware iteration side? Because I know you want like the most reliable system, but you also have to be improving the technology in the, you know, at the same time. Um, how have you kind of married the two over time to be able to do as many deliveries as possible while also cycling in new builds? >> Yeah, I mean that's like that's the main trick. Uh, especially when it comes to this kind of an you know, in this industry, automated logistics, drone delivery. I mean, no one has any idea what this is going to look like at scale. It's 12:01 a.m. We're figuring everything out completely from scratch. There are no precedents. When you think about it, at least Tesla like knew they were building a car. Like they knew it probably had four wheels and a and a steering wheel, you know, but like no one has any idea what this looks like. um there really is no precedent for like how should charging work, what does the business model look like, how does maintenance work, you know, what's the right regulatory strategy, how reliable does it need to be, how should you price it, no one has any idea. And I I think one of Zipline's advantages was I mean for sure we were like the smallest team. I mean Amazon at the time, right? Jeff Bezos had been on 60 Minutes saying they were going to be doing drone delivery to every home in the US within two years. This was in 2013. And so we always thought, okay, well, we're definitely going to be a fast forward Amazon. They were spending like who knows, you know, hundreds of millions of billion dollars a year funding that effort. And we had, you know, a tiny fraction of that and a tiny team. And we always thought, you know, we were not the biggest team, not the we didn't have the most money, but we always knew for sure like we were the most practical. That was in our DNA. We were extremely practical. We were living in these, you know, construction trailers out here, working nights and weekends, and we thought that our DNA should be like we could move super fast in the real world and get things into the real world quickly. And so, the whole trick is that when it comes to hardware systems like this, safety is obviously paramount. I mean, you can look at Uber ATG or you look at Cruise, I mean, you can see the impact of a really bad safety event on the company's trajectory. And and it was especially important because Zipline was delivering a life-saving product to someone whose life was like hanging in the balance. And yet we knew we need to move really fast. And so really the answer to that is like being extraordinarily good at testing. Uh Zipline invested a lot not just in this large team that was doing software in the loop testing, but we also built these really um powerful HDL systems. HDL stands for hardware in the loop testing. Um we learned a lot from SpaceX on that front. SpaceX maybe the best company in the world at Hiddle. And uh we we learned a lot from them and built these complicated hill systems that meant that we could deploy new software onto the actual avionics of the aircraft which is then connected to a simulator. So the plane thinks it's flying even though it's basically sitting on a desk is how to think about it. and is a really powerful thing because it means that 95% of software bugs we can catch without sending something to a test site and accidentally crashing an aircraft due to some stupid software. >> I'd imagine it's way more expensive and way more time intensive if you have the ladder. >> Yes. But even so, 5% of the problems you can only catch at the test site, which is why Zipline has invested a lot of money and time and resources into building these uh large test sites that operate 24/7 in the US. We have three major test sites. One just opened last week. Um, these test sites enable us to get access to a ton of different real world conditions that would be impossible to replicate in simulation. And some stuff you are only going to see it break in the real world. You know, you're looking for the really weird edge cases of like some bird nesting in some part of the hardware or, you know, um, like solar weather for example. You know, solar weather wound up being a pretty big consideration for us in terms of getting the GPS reliability that we needed, the number of nines we needed. solar weather winds up being like a really major consideration. Something that most normal people don't even know that much about but like is really important for these kinds of systems. >> Was the fact that the like weather in Rwanda is so bad and there's like lightning storms and all this sort of thing just like the perfect environment for >> we were complete idiots and we had no idea. You didn't know at the time? No, we we had no idea. Like we launched in Rwanda with zero context on like what we were getting ourselves into. Had we had any, you know, ounce of common sense, we would have like looked at the weather forecast before we signed that contract. I don't think we did. Also, Rwanda is actually a very high elevation country. Um, which means it's like they're just >> it has some of the most volatile country weather on Earth of any country. It has challenging terrain. I mean, there were a lot of things that were really challenging about Rwanda, but it was hyperravity training. Like, we had to build the system in a way that 10 million plus people could rely on with their lives and the lives of their kids. And obviously failure was not an option. And so we just had to make the system we had to harden the system to these extreme weather events. And you know that work has obviously become immensely valuable to Zipline over time because Zipline is now the only autonomy company that operates at this scale in all these gnarly weather conditions day in and day out 24/7. But we were sort of like forced into that by the nature of the original use case that we chose. I think Antonio was interviewing you at some point and he said that you guys are basically the only company that was able to just collect all the data through just doing all these tests. I think it was like 40 million miles autonomous miles flown before you even started doing commercial operations in the US or even sounds right. Yeah. 40 million 50 million. >> What was it like when the FAA came over there and basically went and talked with the uh authority in Rwanda and basically talked to them about potentially, you know, working with you guys on the US operation? What was that like? Well, we always I mean, you know, the big vision here was to build an automated logistics system for Earth. We think that in the same way that the internet can move things back, move information back and forth very efficiently no matter where you are in the world. We thought, man, logistics, it's like all built on top of humans and gas combustion vehicle and unions and it's just this like very complicated antiquated system in certain ways. And we kind of felt like ultimately this is all just going to be automated. It's going to be dramatically less expensive, dramatically faster. And of course, the advantage of that is that logistics today only does a good job of serving like maybe the golden billion people on Earth. But if you're not in the golden billion, there's 7 billion humans who are not in the golden billion. And those seven billion people, their access to logistics either sucks or is non-existent. And so, you know, we felt like by far the most exciting thing here would be to build a logistic system that could serve all people equally. like that was the promise of automation and robotics being applied to this problem. And so in 2019 when the US I mean it wasn't the FAA I mean it was really a lot of these big US companies u a lot of big US health systems were seeing what we were doing saying like hey we have all these similar problems we're trying to advance teleresence >> um >> and we need ways of delivering directly to customer homes faster um or we need ways of meeting like home healthcare nurses where they are at um and then we're also hearing from big companies like Walmart which is you know was been one of our main partners in the us that they wanted to be able to enable teleportation from their stores directly to homes. And we were hearing from a lot of these amazing restaurants that we work with like um Chipotle or Sweet Green or Menesino Farms or Wendy's and um and they also wanted to be able to do teleportation directly to customer homes. And so and and and again and some of the biggest health systems like um like Cleveland Clinic in the US and Michigan Medicine and Memorial Herman, they were all like really really excited to launch. And so it kind of became this issue of like, well, we got to get regulatory permission. And the FAA didn't have a precedent for how to do this. I think it's just a really challenging chicken and the egg problem because they, you know, drone delivery companies had been asking the FAA to do this, but the FAA was like, "Cool, prove that it's safe." And these companies were like, "Well, we can prove that it's safe once you let us operate." >> It's the catch 22. >> Yeah, it's a catch22. And so, Zipline had this big advantage, which is we had 40 or 50 million commercial autonomous miles outside the US with zero safety incidents that we were able to provide to the FAA and say, "Here's how you know it's safe." And that wound up being really, really valuable. And so, um, you know, the FAA was able to move, um, pretty quickly like we, you know, we've always kind of had this strategy of just being an amazing partner to them. Like the FAA wants to innovate. um especially all the people I would say doing the actual like individual contributor work like they want to win uh they're often constrained by like crazy legal bureaucracy and just these like crazy processes and rules and you know don't get me started on NEPA and all these things that actually prevent f federal agencies from doing what they want to do but um we ended up creating this really productive partnership with the FAA over five years and Zipline has now been able to basically like year over year over year kind of like push the envelope, expand what's possible. Um, and the FAA has been our partner every step of the way in making that happen. So, I think that, you know, maybe there was this narrative that like, oh, the FA is going to be impossible or it's like too bureaucratic, it's not going to get the job done. I mean, it's like it's been the exact opposite over the last three to four years. Like, there is no regulatory constraints on Zipline's business right now. Um, because once we were able to show that the thing was safe, the FAA, you know, was a partner in accelerating this. I was talking with Denton beforehand and he mentioned basically flying to Rwanda recently and he was like having trouble with the border patrol or something and then the guy heard that he worked a zipline and there was just like this smile that came across his face and he's like a you know go right through no problem. um if you deliver a million bags of blood or vaccines or so on, maybe you're only touching a million people's lives, but then you have this like ripple effect where there's not only, you know, it's a million people with families, right? And so you create a bunch of like customer love and goodwill with with an entire like nation honestly um by by creating something that's super valuable for a single person. How do you think about just like kind of creating the right customer brand and and like love for drones in in general uh in your mind? I mean, yeah. And by the way, you know, we've now expanded from Rwanda to Ghana, Nigeria, Kotivoir, Kenya, um, and and then obviously the US and, and um, we'll we'll be announcing new countries next year, too. But like I think that in all of these countries, I mean, yeah, you're definitely right. If you if you spend time in these countries, like you will for sure meet people who are alive because of Zipline or they have loved ones who are alive because of Zipline. And like you hear stories all the time and so like you know people at Zipline they'll be in like cabs and they'll be talking to someone they're like oh yeah my sister my mom my son my grandpa whatever like there are a lot of stories I mean zipline now um you know these global public health institutions estimate that zipline I mean University of Pennsylvania did this big study showing that we've been able to reduce the maternal mortality rate in Rwanda by 51%. Um, in fact, the minister of health of Rwanda was just named to the Times 100 list this year for improvements to maternal mortality the country achieves. It's an incredible. >> Do you know how many like lives that's saved? Like if you had to >> many thousands. Yeah. I mean, we, you know, these other big studies like estimated that Zipline is saving about 17,000 lives a year. Now, we're about to triple or quadruple the scope and scale of like our platform one infrastructure in the world as part of this big contract with the US State Department. Um, and so that'll go from 17,000 lives a year to 60,000 lives a year. Um, yeah, that that that's definitely a huge part of why we do what we do. And it's why like logistics, I mean, people often think of logistics as like a luxury for the rich. And the reality is it is is much more than that, you know. It is it is a necessity for for the poor and uh and instant delivery particularly is like a necessity. um like the less time you have in your life to you know drive yourself to somewhere or that you have access to like get to that good hospital or get to that place or you know show up for that uh appointment um you know being able to meet people where they are is incredibly important from an econ from an economic perspective and from a healthcare perspective. So um anyway as we've expanded to all these countries like I think that one observation is that obviously that's really really kind of critical for the brand of the company and for like community acceptance where we operate. If you ask people a lot of times they'll say like, "Oh yeah, like our drones save lives. Our drones deliver these blood transfusions." So I think there's like an extremely strong sense of local ownership. That's because Zipline hired entirely local teams. We created all these really highpaying jobs of like, you know, flight operators and fulfillment operators who ran the distribution centers. And then we promoted those people into positions of global leadership and global executive leadership over time as they were like, you know, proved themselves and and so I think there's this very strong sense that this is not like an American company or or a African company. It's it's a global company. Yeah. >> And anybody in the company can rise super fast if they are extraordinarily talented. And if you're in San Francisco, I think the perhaps sad reality is that the places that have like the most access to technology are the places that appreciate it the least and in fact are often the places that have these really strong like nimi or kind of like anti-tech, anti-progress strains and ideologies. You can see like the coning of autonomous cars in some of these cities and like you know there's this I mean Whimo just hit a cat, right? >> Like ban Whimo now. >> Yeah. There's this like big New York Times article about how like they should ban Whimo and obviously only at the very end of the article do they mention that Whimo is not is is 30 times less likely to hit a cat than a human [laughter] driver, you know? So it's like do we care about the cats, guys, or is you know something else going on here? I think the reality is that like some of these cities um have had have had it so good and get so much tax revenue from technology companies and from this kind of like technology boom that they actually have become pretty negative on it and maybe just think that like no matter how hard they try to strangle the golden goose like it'll always be there. But like the reality is 99% of the world isn't that they're dying for innovation, dying for a tech startup to show up and help them solve problems in a really innovative way. They are dying for entrepreneurship and technology and high-paying jobs. And there are entrepreneurs in all of these countries and in all these other parts of the US that are dying for an opportunity to work on something really cool. you have only to open the door a single inch and these kinds of people will do the rest. And I think that that's kind of been the story of Zipline. It's like we went to parts of the world that people, you know, ignore. And we did that outside the US and then we even did that inside the US. You know, when we launched in the US, we launched in Bentonville, Arkansas. And then we launched in Dallas. And we're going to announce a lot of other metros that people don't think of as like technology is the top place, but then you know >> San Francisco, New York, >> um Boston, you know. So anyway, I think that is the cool thing about um I think a lot of you know this next generation AI and robotics technology is that like a lot of this stuff requires operating in the real world. And when you have to operate in the real world, you actually have to do it in partnership with government. You have to do it in partnership with regulators. you have to do in partnership with cities. And that is going to lead, I think, more companies to do what Zipline did and go and find government partners that are as aggressive and startup oriented as they are and like really want to um create change and move super fast. They exist. In fact, they might be the majority. It's just that they're not the metros that most people think about when they think about advanced technology. >> Yeah. If you had to like go back and you were given the same set of cars, but you have the knowledge that you have today on how you built a business and survived, um, what would you have done differently? >> It's hard to know the counterfactual. I think Zipline got incredibly lucky at a lot of key moments. I you know, always like the biggest mistake you make is is around hiring. Like I think probably everybody has to learn these hard lessons for themselves. is like you can hear it but you have to eventually go make the mistake. But I think that the biggest mistake that I made on hiring again and again was that I would fall in love with specific experience >> over innate characteristics. And whenever we hired for specific experience over innate characteristics, it was a huge mistake. The reality is like a startup that's moving super fast and rapidly iterating like you'll hire someone and then you they'll have a role and have a job description but you know two months later it's like oh my god everything's changed new direction new strategy now we need you to work on this completely different thing. If you hired someone because they were like just super super deep in this one thing they're instantly going to be irrelevant as soon as the company is like changing and the company's changing direction so often. Whereas we realized that there were these like four innate characteristics of people who when we hired them, they practically couldn't. It's like we couldn't get it wrong. Like these people would always end up winning at Zipline. And these four innate characteristics were practical problem solver, fast learner, low ego, missiondriven. Those were things that we felt like were very hard to teach someone. Like we didn't really think we could teach it to people. But if we could hire people who who had those 4NA characteristics, you know, they might fail a couple times while they were figuring out how to do the thing, but eventually they would succeed. And that was um it's crazy how many times I had to make that mistake. >> What were the like most memorable and how did you kind of like shift that over time towards just focusing on these four things? >> I mean, every single bad hire you make is extremely memorable. Um firing people is no fun. And also, you know, when you're trying to lead a startup, you're having to constantly ask everybody to like believe in you. You know, you're like, "Okay, everybody, I have this great idea. We're going to hire this person and they're going to be absolutely amazing for all these reasons." And so, you know, they're going to lead this team and these four people are going to report to them and like it's all your credibility. And so then three months later or six months later, you're like, "All right, guys. Bad news. That didn't work out at all. I was a complete idiot, but now I've learned my lesson. and now we're going to do this new thing. I mean, actually like leading a startup is kind of shameless. You know, you're making all these stupid mistakes and having to like constantly ask the team to okay, now you should believe in me because now I have the right idea. Now we're going to go do this other thing. And um yeah, I think it is it is kind of shameless in that way. Uh but I do love this line of uh I think I I forgot exactly where it's I don't think it's from startups, but it's like um winning is just going from loss to loss or failure to failure without a loss of enthusiasm. Mhm. Maybe. Uh yeah, I mean there was a lot of losing and we hired a lot of people who didn't work out. And I think that you know to me those are all like the if you if I look at the mistakes where I'm like oh man you know the things I could have done differently. It's all around like how could we have bet bigger on the people who have wound up you know being at Zipline for a decade and have grown by leaps and bounds and become executives in the company. How could we have, you know, you want to hire as many people like that as you possibly can and then um just treating hiring like a sacred duty and like no amount of time spent with a candidate is too much time because the cost of getting the decision wrong is so is so high. So I think >> the leverage of getting it right is so high. >> Yeah. And and so those are the biggest like when I look back those are always like the biggest things. cuz it's like could you have moved faster to build the team but I don't know maybe everybody has to learn those lessons for themselves. >> How are you kind of deciding when the right time to scale up is whereas you know just focusing on the thing that you're currently doing and getting it absolutely right um is the best focus. I think that largely we've just like listened to the market. You know, for five or six years, it was kind of clear to us that the US probably wasn't open to us from a regulatory perspective. We just needed to prove what we were doing. We were expanding um all of our kind of like healthcare logistics business outside the US. Uh and we we had our hands full. But in 2020, I think it started to feel like, okay, we could see 3 or 4 years into the future, never more than that, but like 3 or 4 years, it felt like automated logistics was going to dramatically accelerate. And we felt like if we could build a product that would really compete against cars in every way, be dramatically better, that we had enough regulatory progress that we could get regulatory permission. this would work in in every country including in the US. I think we kind of had a good enough understanding of the problems. We also had these customers telling us what the problems were. We had big partners like Walmart willing to sign big contracts with Zipline to provide this kind of technology. And so, you know, at every stage you're kind of like 10xing or 100xing and you're having to build hardware that is dramatically more cost-ffective, dramatically more reliable, dramatically higher performance. like it's usually every product is much more complicated than the last. Um you can look at like SpaceX going from Falcon 1 to Falcon 9 to Starlink and then Starship. Um like we, you know, platform 2 is like 10 times as complicated as the original version, platform one, um of the technology that we built. And uh yeah, as I mentioned, like it almost killed us trying to get that product into the real world. But the thing was like we we knew that the the market on the other side was like incredibly vast. It's like one of the biggest markets on Earth already, instant delivery. And we actually think that if you can make it well actually we now have data showing that if you make it 10 times as fast and less expensive and way more reliable and safe for customers to use, they'll consume like 10 times as much of it. So we actually believe that the instant delivery market that you see in in the US today, for example, 5 a half billion instant deliveries being made every year, it's actually very obvious that there's demand for 50 billion instant deliveries. And there's definitely, you know, not enough roads, not enough cars, not enough humans who want to do that job in the United States to deliver 50 billion uh deliveries a year. But that's where the world is going. And so we need to do it using technology that is, you know, that that actually takes cars off the road that gives humans highpaying like next tech ne, you know, nextgen technology jobs rather than, you know, low wage minimum jobs that people basically as soon as they can get any other job, they they abandon it and take it. And we got to do it in a way that's quiet and serene and beautiful for neighborhoods and like reduces pollution in our neighborhoods. >> Yeah. >> And so that's that's the vision. And I think that it's only really this year that it's become clear. I mean, looking at the way that Zipline has been scaling in the US, it's only now that it's suddenly clear that that's all going to happen over the next, you know, three to five years. >> What was kind of the day where you woke up and you were like, "This is going to work." >> I don't know. Last week. >> Last week. >> Maybe maybe I haven't had that day yet, honestly. I mean, you live in a state of constant paranoia and, you know, [clears throat] I don't think I ever tell myself like it's definitely going to work, but I definitely think there have been eyebrow raising moments over the last couple weeks. You know, we are hitting like new record numbers of deliveries practically every day. And if you just look at like platform 2 deliveries in the US since about April, we've been growing about 15% week overw week. And so when you look at that on a graph, I mean, it is like super vertical. Like we're in a completely vertical part of the graph. And it made me, it makes you realize when you're getting into like thousands of deliveries a day, tous thousands of deliveries a day, it's like this is even though it's still a small scale compared to UPS or FedEx, this is this is something that is going to grow a lot and it is going to be completely normal for our neighborhoods. I mean, I went and visited a grandma in Bentonville. She's 82 years old. She's ordered from Zipline 350 times in the last year. Uh, you know, there are the service right now has a net promoter score of 95, >> which I don't know if I've ever heard of a >> Yeah, I mean normal delivery platforms actually have >> net promoter scores of like negative -10 -20. Um, yeah, a lot of these delivery platforms have scaled really really fast with negative with net detractor customer bases. It's kind of an amazing thing and I think it's honestly a testament to the to the opportunity and the scale of the market like how much people want this is that like they're willing to use a service that's actually like super expensive and pretty unreliable and sometimes unsafe and where the experience of the product is usually bad where the food is like >> ice cold and gross and doesn't taste nearly as good but like you know even so these platforms have scaled to extraordinary scale. I mean, it's definitely a testament to the operational um you know the operational knowhow and performance of of those of those delivery platforms. But ultimately it seems very obvious that these systems need to be automated. Like we should not be using technology that's 100 years old to solve a problem that like a a brand new industry that we've only really understood in the last 5 or 10 years. like basically to solve these problems of instant delivery to solve these problems of um global logistics we think we should be using technology you know AI and robotics are going to transform that and that's the sort of simple idea that zipline has been pursuing for 12 years and when we are now seeing customers adopt the service adopt um the the you know and and ordering everything through the zipline app It's kind of crazy to see the user behaviors. I mean, people are, you know, people are ordering at their offices, people are ordering at universities, we deliver to apartments, we would deliver to town homes, deliver to hotels, we've even delivered to public parks. And I think people realizing that you can just pull out your phone and press a couple but a delightful experience. >> Yeah. Have something delivered quietly and precisely and safely 10 to 15 minutes later, no matter what your GPS coordinates are, um, is a it definitely changes the way that people live. Like people change their grocery shopping behaviors and we talk to families who are like, "Oh yeah, now we we grocery shop like once a week and then we do three to four fill-in orders with zipline a week and so it enables them to like eat healthier food, you know, they can yeah, they can eat fresher food. They can um they waste less because they don't have to batch process and like plan ahead." Um, and most importantly, it's like saving these families three to five hours a week that they don't have to be battling in traffic, um, parking in a parking lot, dragging kids, kicking and screaming into a store to get something. It's like everything is delivered to you. So, it's like 3 to 5 hours you have to just enjoy time with your kids or loved ones rather than fight in traffic or in a store.

5KNQeZ_95O0.title: How micro1 grew from $4M to $200M revenue in a year | Ali Ansari
5KNQeZ_95O0.text: I came to the realization that the founders's job is to inject as much risk as they can into the company because really no one else will. >> Like upside risk, >> bold moves that have pretty bad downside also, but if it works, it works [music] well. What I focus pretty much entire time on is three things. One is hiring, two is product, and three is aligning incentives. You have to align incentives very long term. When you're in a space that is growing so fast and 3 months can literally double your run rate, you actually do need short-term incentives. Our recruitment team sometimes gets these like absurd bonuses if they hire like a thousand people. You know, sometimes we have a customer that is about to sign. I might tell them like, "Hey, if this closes, you'll double your equity." Today I have the pleasure of sitting down with Ali Ansari. He is the founder and CEO of Micro One. Let's start off with [snorts] I think a lot of the models today are kind of like specific versions like GBT, you know, four or five so on. Um, but eventually we're going to have like these constantly updated models. You specifically are trying to create like the super specific high-quality data for constantly improving models all the time. Can you just talk about that? >> Yeah, absolutely. Well, first of all, it's good to be here. Thanks for having me, Ty. Um so I think you know the way that AI labs are improving their models is by picking domains to improve on and uh you know creating reward models within those domains through this notion of RL environments and connecting their policy model which is the the model that kind of serves the the customers and uh improving in that domain of choice. And you know the some domains are emergent which means as you improve in any that domain coding is one example there's a lot of kind of other functionalities that that come about um that are beyond coding capabilities but but the truth is most domains are actually not so emergent. If you improve in any given domain you have to you'll just improve in that domain. And so what this means is that labs are you know having to essentially pick a very wide range of domains your finance, medical, legal and very long tail hundreds of other domains to to build general intelligence and the way the way that they do so is they um create these you know expert level kind of data sets that then results in a reward model which then connects to their policy model to improve it. So that's kind of the that's kind of the you know the structure where researchers come up with these hypotheses of like hey I think this data structure is going to work. I think hopefully it will be emergent but in most cases it won't be and then uh it uh you know then they they kind of gather that data uh by having a bunch of experts create the net new data that's required to improve the capabilities. >> You started off with just a recruiting tool for great talent. But what is the process that you go through before you decide we're going to hire someone new versus just you know you have more leverage figure out how to do it. >> Yeah. So so the first thing is we first see if we can actually delete that thing that they're constrained by >> like the question the requirement. >> Yeah. Exactly. Like there's a recent case of um this like very frankly bad process of sending out contracts to experts and this required a lot of human involvement and therefore one of the teams that does this had to kind of hire a bit faster than others and you know I I looked into like what why there's why is this team kind of growing pretty quickly and then we realized that we actually just should not have this this requirement of uh signing contracts in this spent about a week with the engineering team to kind of automate this function which of course freed up this team's time to do much better things and we didn't need to hire for for that at all and uh we slowed it right away and so I think there's a lot of kind of questioning the requirements as as Elon puts it really well that has to happen um and then I think the other thing is like if that requirement must be there and that function must exist just really dive in and see if folks are actually not getting enough time to do it or if they're busy with some other, you know, coordination or something that that exists and then then they're not able to do the core function that they're supposed to do and try to delete those functions to to allow them to actually focus on that one thing that that that must exist. When things are growing as fast as they are right now, how do you kind of plan a few months in advance or even like a year or two out and try to predict where the business will be and how can we build everything today so that you're basically ready for when that point hits. >> Honestly, we don't plan. [laughter] >> What do you mean by that? >> We we we try to do a little bit of planning and then it just ends up being, you know, we have to kind of replan similar to how the KPIs have to be kind of readjusted so many times. And and I think, you know, eventually we'll get out of this state of like just complete outlier 30x year-over-year type growth. And, you know, it will eventually normalize a bit more. And that's that's the the simple truth. And at that point, there will be more of a typical planning and budgets and so forth. But right now, when you know, when the when like when the board says like what's the the budget planning for the year? I mean, I I haven't even looked at what that means. Like, we're we're uh I actually don't even know how to like structure a budget yet. Like, we'll look into that later. we we are you know we we've been profitable so like the budget is fine so you know once we become like not profitable and can plan a bit more then I'll look into like what it means to set up a budget [laughter] and and we'll do it at that point so there's not much planning that happens >> yeah I also I I kind of love that um not very many companies especially this early on are able to get to a point where they are default not only default alive where you have enough cash in the bank to get to profitability but you're already there um and what has that kind of enabled you to do like just psychologically dayto-day when you're thinking about business decisions, you know, you have a great business and it's growing really fast. How are you kind of thinking about growing it even further after you've reached that point? >> Yeah. So, I I think it's uh you know, obviously we're very grateful to be in this position of profitability. We um we've been profitable for a while now. pretty much almost entirely 2025 was profitable and this actually resulted in us being um net profitable historically now which means we've the amount that we've raised we have not touched it and we've added to it. >> Um so you know feel obviously feels really good to be in this position and be able to kind of determine our own on our own faith and not not be able to uh if if we don't want to not have to raise money. Um but but I think at the same time there's a lot of uh there's a lot of good stuff to spend on and and we want to you know part of it is just this idea of like proactively building pipelines and for us to have material revenue come from those pipelines the cost basis needs to be high. I mean if we want a pipeline to give us u tens of millions of dollars in in revenue the cost basis needs to be some order of magnitude that needs to be tens of millions of dollars. And so that sort of spend can only happen if we have a a really nice cash cushion. So that's why we we are going to raise. But we um but but we'll we'll try to be very capital efficient still. And and basically the only line item on the P&L that'll grow really fast will be R&D, which is again this like proactive data spend. And everything else we'll try to keep as stable as possible. And I think there's a there's a chance even post our next raise and post spending a lot on on these these pipelines, we may stay profitable and and and it won't be it won't really be the goal. I mean, I think it's it's wrong to like fully optimize for profitability right now, but I think it will be the byproduct of just having discipline within the company and and having this like insane growth. Um, so you know, we'll we'll sort of we'll look at it as a bit of a bit of a side aim, but but not like a full focus. >> Is this something where it's a little bit like Google where DeepMind could not do what they're doing if Google didn't just have this mass massive cash cow, which is search and ads. Um, and because of that, they're able to take all these other bets. Like I look at Deep Mind and they're working on completely different things than other AI labs are. You know, they're working on like protein folding and and stuff like that. Um, and part of part of the reason that they're able to do that is because they've got this just massive cash generation engine which they know is going to be there pretty much forever. How is that kind of allowed you to take or is going to allow you to take different bets? >> Yeah, I think obviously DM is an incredible company. No, no way that we can compare oursel in any way. But I think there's this idea of just having a large cash cushion gives you the flexibility to take on big bold bets. And you know, we we believe that this market will be a multi- trillion dollar a year spend market on on on human data over the long run. But of course, there's a chance that that's wrong and there's a chance that like maybe the data business actually doesn't work. And if we have that cash cushion, we'll do everything we can to make it work and be the biggest winner in the space. But if it doesn't, we also have time to make sure that that the current state of our product gets applied to something else. So, so that that that kind of um you know, comfort is not the right word, but but that but that cash cushion in general allows us to just really take the bold bets that are required to hopefully build a multiund billion dollar company. >> You posted a blog post probably like two weeks ago now and you basically said here's why training data and like human data is just going to continue to be valuable over time. Um, do you want to walk through what that actually looks like? Like why can human data just be like a trillion dollar industry long term? >> Yes, it's uh I have a lot of thoughts on this, so cut me off if I'm talking too much here, but I so there's a there's there's a bunch of things. First is there's this notion of last mile in AI. And I think the first thing here is that the last mile in AI just doesn't exist. The reason for it is, you know, right now the the strategy for labs, what what people perceive that the strategy is that they're kind of trying to automate and and and optimize the last 10% of capabilities. But, you know, in some ways that's that's true, but in other ways the capabilities that exist currently won't be the same ones that exist in the future. Like the function space of the economy will expand very largely and rapidly >> with I mean with every techn technological revolution the function space of the economy expands rapidly but especially with this one it will expand because humanity will have its time freed up on hopefully all of the current functions that they do which means all of the current functions of the economy will be automated over time and and of course this won't be an instantaneous thing it'll be a very long iteration over time which means as that happens in a continuous manner. Human time gets freed up in every in every domain and they get to spend time on things that are more creative more fun and what that results in is net new functions that get created within those domains. So the example I'll give you that that makes this concrete is um you know obviously an area that we're very familiar with which is recruitment. We um we we believe that Micro One has the most powerful recruiters on the planet currently because of the agent that we built and they're able to hire hundreds of people every single day. And their functions as recruiters does not look the same as any other recruiter at all. They're still recruiters. That job still is there and it's now way more impactful, but the tasks they do looks fundamentally different. And it's it's ones that it's it's a lot more fun for for for these recruiters to work on tasks that they do versus uh tasks that that typical recruiters do. >> It's almost like the uh farmer pre pre and post industrial revolution where you're like actually on a farm versus you're just controlling a bunch of tractors. >> Yeah. And you can 100x that across every function of the economy if if intelligence actually does become quoteunquote commoditized. So, so, so now take this recruitment example and um you know take take what's happening literally today at Micron which is our recruiters are coming up with new things to do that are really impactful that are now part of their function space if you will and and and now we're going after and automating those functions and this loop will continue and and and so I'll give you one you know one uh one specific example in terms of this recruitment function space is that our recruiters because they don't do any interviews anymore and our our agent does all of it. >> They're able to spend time on this like creative sourcing strategies where they create these like fun like almost like marketing campaigns and they're almost like doing kind of a marketing job in a recruitment context where recruiters would not do that before and so this is a net new function that has create that hasn't been created and it's a lot more fun for humans that will automate also but then then there'll be nuance. So, so that's the first thing which is there is no last mile and so as humanity comes up with new functions we will have to create we we would have to get we will have to get structured human judgment on those net new functions to then automate those functions. So that's that's first. The second thing is um which is like you know maybe maybe even a bigger reason which is the labs and everyone just broadly in the US and pretty much all around the world is spending a lot of money on compute buildouts and of course algorithm efficiencies like hiring researchers and so forth but mainly compute buildouts like hundreds of billions of dollars maybe like trillion dollars at this point and they're they're betting on future inference and as Jensen says a lot inference is getting a million or billion X or something. And for that to happen and for for the for like for the economy to not collapse entirely because of all these buildouts, we need to unlock a lot of new capabilities for models and and it but it the inference will the current state of models that inference will not be used at all. Like very small portions of what the future bet inference is will be used. And so we must unlock a lot of new capabilities. and and the way to do so again is structured human judgment in each of those domains that we're trying to unlock uh AI uh capabilities in and there's no other there's no other route. The third thing is as synthetic data becomes more and more relevant and useful. What that results in is every human data point becomes a lot more valuable because you're if you think about like the current pipelines what happens in pretty much every pipeline is you take some amount of human structure judgment and you you extrapolate that by a lot with synthetic data. Of course, it's not in every pipeline, but like in most pipelines, there's some notion of increasing the data points by orders of magnitude, sometimes like like thousandx with with with synthetic data and and if synthetic data generation becomes even better and you're able to like you know millionx that or something and like the model can train on way less human data points, that's actually the greatest thing that can happen to our to our business and the human data market which like is a you know maybe the most bas basic economic principle which is if something is more valable a lot more of it will be will be there there will be demand for and and and so that the spend will increase by orders of magnitude and so we we hope synthetic data continues to be uh continues to accelerate and capabilities and we actually want to like contribute to that uh as well um and so so these are kind of the three main things that will result in uh this massive market and actually sorry last thing I'll say is um there's if you think about like a let's take one example of Um lawyers lawyers what they do at their in in you know in their job is they create basically unstructured data for their law firm all day right they do they they redline some random contract they they get paid for it they they do some M&A they get paid for it and it's about a lot of unstructured work happening which obviously is very useful for the economy I mean clearly but but you have to wonder why lawyers are as just one example are getting paid more to work at micro one than their law firm They're getting roughly 20% more. And it's not I mean, of course, it's us paying them more, but it's like the economy that like allows for that. >> The value that they're creating is just literally higher. >> Exactly. A and specifically the structured redlininings that they do, the structured M&A tasks that they create are the economy has determined are more valuable than the unstructured work they do for their law firm. Mhm. >> And so so one natural argument is like okay so why don't they just spend their whole time then like clearly but but but you can't do that because there needs to be some percentage to to actually run the economy until we automate it. Um and so there needs to be some like equilibrium point of spending some portion of their time on structured value creation and then and then unstructured. But but you could infer from this uh argument that I if if the current state of basically every domain is some percentage of their time is spent on structured human judgment and human data creation then over time basically the entire economy will spend some small percentage of their time on this idea of human data and and so you could take a percentage of the entire labor market and and put it as human data spend. So even if you take 5% and which we have, you know, I did some math on like why I think 5% is reasonable. If you take 5% of 50 trillion a year in spend, that's $2.5 trillion a year and and then you just discount it by a lot for for whatever reason, you know, maybe not all of it will be kind of like recognized in terms of like spend and a lot of it will happen in like a less formal ways, then you can you can make a pretty clear uh argument for one trillion a year in human data spend over the long run. This almost reminds me of kind of like the uh the safe and when the safe was invented. There was all these very complex uh instruments that startups used to be funded on and then I think what was it Carolyn Levy invented the safe and it suddenly enabled founders to basically raise a huge amount of money in a very short period of time simply simply because there wasn't necessarily as many steps in the process. It didn't cost as much. It wasn't as complex. >> Yeah. I think in that case you would maybe argue that because it's so much easier to raise and and maybe a little bit easier to also invest that somehow there will actually be less VCs. I mean certainly not there there's a lot more and that you know I think that has a good impact on the economy. Um, so yeah, I think it's similar arguments the the kind of the the principle of Jevans paradox >> I want to spend a little time on. You mentioned I think that you're basically getting more and more data points that are like long horizon tasks. Uh, and you're trying to have a single person maybe spend a week or weeks on a single data point that you know is fed into a model. Um, >> how has that kind of evolved and how did you kind of come to that conclusion? If you think about the current state of models, they're they're very good at answering complex questions in pretty much every domain. No matter how complex the domain is, how niche it is, they will answer your question. And you know, you could put some broad accuracy on the answers of like let's say 90% just generally of of accuracy and they continue to to get better in those as well. And I think we'll approach even higher accuracy just just broadly. But but then if you think about models doing tasks and and what it means to do a task I think for a human what it means to do a task is like you essentially answer a bunch of questions right you you you you first determine you know the first step of a task is you kind of planning it so the question is like what is the plan for this task right and then like you you take an action and you're just like answering the question of what the next action should be and like you're essentially just answering a series of questions and then obviously you're making some movements to actually act on it. So what the models have to do is uh basically just answer a bunch of questions in a row to do tasks. And answering a question with 90% accuracy is a is a good outcome. But if you have to answer 20 questions in a row to do a task and you do 0.9 to the power of 20, you're going to get like I don't know close to zero like 0.15 or something like that. some some very low number of accuracy and which means you're going to have uh that task be done correctly like literally 10% of the time and that's obviously horrible. And so this idea of like compounding errors is why models are not yet good at doing much and and even for applications that are like coding and and they're obviously making a lot of great impact. If if you if you ask cursor to kind of go back in the conversation and like check something you asked a few turns ago, there's some struggles, right? And this so this this idea of like multi-step tasks and very long horizon tasks is is what models continue to struggle with. And and so the way to get them to not struggle is by um not creating tasks that are questions and answers but creating tasks that are actual tasks that that have that are very long horizon. you know, one um one example would be like if you look at the domain of taxes um and you know, currently if you ask anything about like W2 California taxes, you'll get great responses, but there isn't really an agent that like will file your taxes. So what we're trying to do is like one of the RL environments that we're building is um essentially simulating the full endto-end process of filing one's taxes which is certainly complex and it's not just about the final tax form that you fill out. It's about first getting the right information from the customer. The customer is going to probably tell to you like hey can you do my taxes and not send you any information and then you're going to say hey like can you send your income for the year send your bank statements and few other things. they'll send you half of what you asked and then you'll have to ask again and then you'll uh you know you you'll probably need some more information because they had some capital gains and so forth. So the first step is like a bunch of tasks to actually gather the right info and then in the context of again in the context of taxes you then have to kind of have a conversation with the customer about like optimizing their taxes. You certainly don't want to get the information and then file a tax. you got to like say, "Hey, maybe um maybe go buy a car or something and and like reduce your uh or increase your expenses and you know what, whatever. Maybe sell some stocks and have some realized losses to to uh match uh to reduce the realized gains or whatever it is." And and then so so there's like a bunch of questions to be answered in that in that conversation. And then and then there's a bunch of other steps and then and then ultimately you file the taxes which is you take all that previous states that exist and then you you you fill out the form that is sent to the government and and so if you don't have a very long horizon task that kind of gives you rewards that are that are partial rewards for each of those states before the final action of filing one's PD filling out one's PDF you will fail quite badly. So, so that's that's kind of the approach we're taking now, which is these like very long horizon tasks that that simulates endtoend workflows. >> A lot of people kind of thought that 2025 was going to be the year of agents and that didn't really happen like like you said, uh because the there are very long horizon tasks and they're complex. It's very difficult for things to really understand what all the steps are to actually execute and and make something happen. How do you think agents are going to happen to the point where we have super useful entities that are able to go execute your will? >> Yeah, I think you're exactly right. I mean, there's there's a lot of uh there's a couple of really good use cases, coding, customer support, and a few others that have been working very well. Um, but I think realistically there hasn't been yet a huge uh adoption within enterprises or just broadly. And I I think part of that is uh part of what people argue is um the fact that enterprises there's just like this lag in the economy to uh adopt new technology generally and I think you know that's partly true but I I would say it's actually mainly because of this um notion of eval is not yet built into enterprises. So the first part is of course you have to improve the foundational models in these long horizon tasks. So that that's that's kind of the the first step. But then the second is um when you actually are using a foundation model to build any agent in any given context for an enterprise, you need to actually really further evaluate that within the workflows within the very niche workflows of that one enterprise. And so this is like this notion of like contextual evaluations which I think enterprises have not yet really thought about or implemented. And I think the way that this adoption uh speed changes is is if it if enterprises start to treat evaluations as core as engineering in their full-on product buildouts. So basically a very large portion of the product budgets within these companies has to be uh spent on evaluations where they're kind of looking at each function that this agent should have needs to be qualitatively assessed versus uh like does it work or not but but how well does it work? >> Final question. What's the hardest thing you've overcome? realizing in retrospect how much my parents gave up to come to the US when they were when they had a pretty good life in Iran. They they they had to um they had to give up pretty much everything and kind of restart their life. And of course, US is definitely the greatest country to be in. But if someone has spent decades in one country and has to reset their life entirely, it's an incredibly difficult thing to do. And you know, I remember the early days of uh my parents like really struggling when they came to the US and us having to kind of live in a single bedroom with a family of four for a long time and all the rest that I I'm now kind of appreciative of in retrospect of of how hard they had to work for me and my sister to basically be able to live here and have a good education and have the opportunity to build companies and so forth. And so I think this is part part of my maybe the main part of my drive is um really making sure that my parents get a great outcome with this move and hopefully I can you know contribute that to that outcome.

JQpwOkDUHvg.title: The Future of Metal Manufacturing | Machina Labs
JQpwOkDUHvg.text: I was talking with some of my friends in Tesla. A full set of dyes for one of the first cars they made was $120 million. With our process, we realized, okay, we can get these robot sheet formers to form sheet metal without any need for dye. Two robots come from two sides, start pinching the sheet. As you expand, one of the robots starts giving it shape. I would love to live in a world where any great idea can become real. I'm a car guy. So, I used to make cars panels with hand. We announced a collaborative work that we're doing with Toyota enabling people to design a car that's uniquely how they want it to look like. If we make a company that enables freedom of expression in physical world, that's going to be a massive company. Today I have the pleasure of sitting down with Ed Mayer and he is the co-founder and CEO of Machina. And Machina is basically building these robots that allow you to form metal in a very unique way. We're sitting next to the I think third or fourth iteration of the France von Holtzhausen [laughter] uh I don't know how you describe that but anyway yeah Fran solo how did that happen? >> Yeah. No so we were going to a conference and the organizers of the conference know say hey in this conference we want to really honor France for all the contribution he has had uh to the auto >> chief designer of Tesla. chief designer of Tesla >> and he's a great guy, fantastic human being. >> And it's like we want to, you know, we want to come come up with something that, you know, really captures him >> in something that's permanent and, you know, and matches his contribution into industry. Obviously, he's built all these amazing metal machines, right? Um, and I was talking to him at the time to the organizer. I was like, "Hey, you know, like we've been doing a lot of sculptures, so maybe we can do something around that." And then he was like, "Yeah, that's a great idea." And then um we chat a little bit and the idea of Han Solo and Fran Solo came about. So uh it shows France being uh memoriz you know kind of memorized in a in a in a in a medal. Um and um there's a picture of us u giving it to him as a gift and uh I think I think he looked happy about it. I know he was being nice but you know it's a pretty pretty massive basically sculpture. when you decided to do this in the first place, like what does that process actually look like from just scanning someone's face or or body to actually having it, you know, made in metal? >> Yeah, this is kind of the comes down to the core thesis of Makita. Can you turn ideas into physical reality as fast as possible in the most automated way, right? So, it starts from a design. Um, we feed the design to our software system. Uh, our software comes up with a rough set of instructions for the robots to manufacture the part. Mhm. >> Um then we send those instructions to the robot. Um the robot looks at the sensor data every 4 millisecond and I make adjustments. So you have the rough instructions but online the robots are constantly make adjustments to make an accurate part in real time. >> So there's both like an offline component and a real-time component >> and then at the end once you're done the robots scans it so we can look at the final result and compare it to what the design was, make any adjustments we need to do or not. The robot can come up actually a set of adjustments to make the part more accurate. Um but the whole um um kind of tool chain from design and idea all the way to the final physical product is part of our technology stack. >> You mentioned when we were just walking around that it used to take like more than 25 attempts before you got a part or you know that was made correctly. How is that how did that start and how has that kind of uh evolved over time? >> Yeah. So what we're doing is something pretty chaotic, right? You know, we call these systems robot craftsman >> because the idea is that they can operate like a human blacksmith or a craftsman. A set of robots, they can pick up different tools, then come up with a set of process parameters and sequence of operations to turn an idea, a design, a CAD into a final product. Uh but a lot goes into that. You know, as a human, we're constantly looking at the piece, making adjustments, changing our course of action. Um so it's a pretty chaotic um high variability kind of process. So we needed to tame that right. So early on we didn't have any data to figure out how we going to tame this process. Right. You know that's one of the challenges >> going from scratch. >> Exactly. In robotic application is that there's not a whole lot of real data out there for you to use to be like okay how am I going to form sheet metal like humans would with a hammer. Um so we first built the machine and the idea was like a human will guide it >> right? So we come up with some kind of heristic set of parameters let the robot do its thing. We look at the results. We make adjustments. go another trial, another trial. So basically it was just human in the loop but instead of human picking up a hammer himself, the robot was picking up the hammer and doing the work and we constantly would look at the data and make adjustments. So early on, you know, one of our early parts we we got was from SpaceX. You know, it was a shroud for a rocket engine and it took us 25 different trials to even get it right. Right. Even ballpark of right. >> And what were all the like issues during those 25 trials? You know like one trial you comes in and the part tears. You know we just apply too much force in one area and the part tears. Another you know you're going through and suddenly the part in the middle the forming buckles because of the stresses. >> Another one you know you you start getting really bad surface quality right digs into the material and it just doesn't look good. Um, and then even if you get the part roughly correct, then it's just inches off of CAT, right? Because the sheet moves in all kinds of unpredictable way as you're forming it. So, a whole bunch of challenges there. Um, so we slowly start adding more and more tool uh so that the human operator can kind of like you know make adjustments that are necessary in a more direct way. Um, and now we're like averaging around four or five trials. The goal is, you know, with enough data, we can build accurate models that like, you know, zero shot it. The first time the robot figures out how to form the part accurately without having to like do any kind of iteration. >> I think this is like a very novel way of forming a part. Um, >> can you talk about how the process actually works from just taking a flat piece of sheet metal to actually having a form part? >> So, forming is our core operation, right? Um, actually the robots do more than forming. They do trimming, they do uh bending, hemming. Those are some of the things we're working on. Heat treatment. >> Did you just start with making forming was a core operation, right? >> But the goal is that the system can do you know anything a human craftsman can do >> because once you have that then you set up a facility you have like 20 30 of these and now you can program that facility to do all kinds of operation to call make all kinds of different different parts. Um but we thought really hard about okay this is a platform that eventually can do any parts but where do we want to start with? Um forming is is one of the I think is the largest metal processing sector. Most of the metal parts you see around you are form sheet metal parts. Um so and the the the traditional way of making formed parts is that you have to make a die u giant stamping presses. These are sometimes like fourstory bu you know building size stamping presses. You put two matching dies, you put a sheet in between them, you apply pressure with the with the stamping press, and you get your part. So, and the bottleneck over there is just those dyes. You know, a you know, I was talking with some of my friends in Tesla a while back. Uh, you know, a full set of dyes for a f one of the first cars they made uh was $120 million, right? So, you're spending significant amount of dollars to to get your first batch of uh die sets, to get your first batch of parts. So with our process we realized okay you know we can get these these robot sheet formers to form sheet metal without any need for dye. Um and the way they work is like similar how to a potter for you know forms a clay bowl. Two robots they have two stylus finger looking things that are super accurate very highly rigid can apply a lot of force. They come and start from a flat sheet that's kind of fixtured in between them. the two robots come from two sides, start pinching the sheet and expanding the sheet as they pinch it. You know, the same way, you know, you get a you get a little, you know, play-doh and you you push it from two sides, it expands. And then as you expand, one of the robots starts giving it shape. So, it deforms it, expands it and gives it shape. And then incrementally over time, you you get to a shape um that is defined through software, just instructions sent to a robot. And there's no static die that is required. Um so complete gets rid of the die a matter of hours after um your design is done you can start getting the first physical part >> for something like this when you are doing like scaled production of you know a single car let's say like the model S >> it makes total sense to invest in that in that die and buy the fourstory building you know thing um >> but for most like iterative you know working on things and and just iterating on a on a designer process like that does not make sense to to make a die for. So what kind of initial products did you guys uh work on? >> Yeah. So yeah the natural fit is where the design constantly changing right you know everybody thinks of our application oh maybe it's a good prototyping you know kind of tool prototyping is great but I think you know we always thought that this could be even go to early production volumes. Um so we look at applications where you know they're making few thousand of each design a year not a million like you would do model Y or some you know Ford F-150 um few thousand a year but then you want to constantly change your designs after like you know you manufacture a few thousand so aerospace becames a very became a very good fit you know the number of airplanes that you know total being manufactured isn't a few thousand a year you know at best you know rockets uh def defense systems Um so those became early early customers.
