So I think one thing that's maybe underappreciated in most startups is like for the most part as a human um organization endeavor right um and I don't think people talk about it as much you know you know technology gets developed you know fundraising eventually gets happen customers come in if you have a useful product u but what makes company great is is the people and how excited about it how mission aligned they are um uh you know the feeling that you get every day you come to the work you know it's not really about the task at least for me the tasks that I do it's about who I'm doing it with you know who are the people that are going to sit down around the table am I going to get challenged by them am I going to have fun working with them um I think those are the stories that are often um not talked about but I think probably the most important stories in the company >> were there any uh examples or do you have any examples of your team because you maybe did this right over time. Um where your team was able to rise to an occasion or make something that you believed wouldn't be possible possible. >> Yeah. I mean exactly you know we made that truck in three months uh and we had like two or three systems we can allocate to it. You know initially it was like uh 50 panels we ended up narrow it down to 13 panels we needed it. The designs need to complete. uh people were working weekends um you know last minute making you know decisions around okay what portion of a technology like you know okay this portion of the feature I'm just going to turn off and not work on and do a manual adjustment as opposed to relying on the on the tech stack. Um, yeah, I think you know it was impossible to get a vehicle from design to full body panels manufactured and assembled in 3 months and we all did it because we had a party coming up and we wanted to showcase the video showcase the vehicle. >> No better reason [laughter] than a party coming up. >> We need to make this happen for the party. Um, you know, and those are the moments that I think you're all going to remember. You know, all the and were not all pleasant, right? you know, uh, some of it was pleasant, some of it was fights, some of it was, you know, people, you know, um, um, um, being frustrated. Um, but then at the end, it all come together, right? >> Yeah. Okay. Final question. >> Yeah. >> What's the hardest thing you've overcome? >> You know, maybe I'll give you an indirect answer and maybe we can kind of dive deeper a little bit and I'll give you any more examples of it. You know, I think startups are probably, you know, building a startup is definitely one of the hardest things. I mean, I've been involved with three startups and just the third one. Um, and first one I'm a CEO in. Um, and it's definitely a hard thing to do. Uh, and I think the hard piece is around it's it's always internal, >> right? It's like something inside there's some resistance you have a lot of time around things when you're wrong. accepting you're wrong. Um um when when something doesn't go your way, start pushing against it and figuring out what you got to do next. I think I guess long story short is I think the hardest things are those internal journeys that you go through. At the end of it, you become a better human as a result, but you you feel a lot of resistance. Um >> what were the biggest moments where you felt that resistance? There are times where you know a business still doesn't come through regardless of how hard you tried. You know you have five six months of runway and you really make sure that you need to get do the next fund raise otherwise you need to let big portion of the team go and you really don't want to hit that deadline. Um and kind of you start going through the scenarios in your head right to be to be ready for it to make all those decisions. I think those are the hard moments. A lot of them don't even happen, but like in your mind, you know, you are you're there. You're like you're making that decision >> and you're like, "Okay, I need to be comfortable with it because it might happen in a month, right?" >> And >> you're running this like uh a whole bunch of universes may unfold and trying to figure out which one you're okay with. You know I it's funny like you know I I did this I had this professor at university um that was was teaching science of decision-m and one of the things you know he was teaching was um you need to be always be positive like you need to be always think about um uh the best outcome and be positive and send the positivity out there and then you'll receive it. I end up I think that didn't really work for me. So what I do, which is kind of the opposite of that, like I'll be like assume the worst >> and and live through the worst situation. >> You've just mentally experienced it and then afterwards you're like, well, it's already experienced it. So >> yeah, and anything that happens is going to be a better situation. But those moments of mentally going through the worst case, >> I think is usually end up being the hardest moment. Um uh and nobody else also knows that's what's going on. It's kind of funny like I I I was thinking about this last night before I went to bed and I'm like the number of things that I think about where I'm like afraid or worried. I don't even know if afraid is the right word, but it's just like worried and thinking about it and I never actually tell anyone else and then I just figure it out the next morning or a day or two later. Ridiculous. Yeah. Yeah. It's it's just ridiculous. And and I I I think you know people have this natural inclination to experience a loss twice as much as as a gain. Um and so we just by definition kind of have to like course correct for that. Yeah. >> Because obviously to you know we're making progress like things are moving forward and you know things aren't actually going to [ __ ] >> Yep. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. And also at the end of the day I think you know you know we're so lucky to be living in the United States you know gone to you know gone through great education you know have the opportunity. I mean like where else you have like venture capital debt financing amazing talent um and the culture that you know >> and then you get you get to like own your work and like own the reward or part of the reward >> and culture that appreciates entrepreneurship and taking risk >> you know at the end of the day I mean we're pretty lucky and uh kind of keeping that in mind that's like you know even the worst case is probably better than most people in the world um um uh it's it's pretty empowering thing to to think

CcLHYxPRjDY.title: Competing With China In 3D Printing | Max Lobovsky, Formlabs
CcLHYxPRjDY.text: Today I'm sitting down with Max Labowski and he is the co-founder and CEO of Formlabs. FormLabs is a 3D printing company. Uh do you want to just start off by actually talking about why there's been so many 3D printing companies and like why yours has been able to be successful? >> Well, the dream of 3D printing is is huge. It's to be able to go from a digital model to a real thing quickly, cheaply, easily. Anyone who's ever made something knows that uh well the bigger problem is going from an idea to a real thing. And anyone who's ever made something in the physical world knows that that is usually a painful long process. And usually your idea gets kind of cut down and limited and um the thing you get out isn't exactly what you imagined. So any tool that that streamlines that process um is is something that's really valuable but also just like conceptually feels amazing. So people have been excited about 3D printers for a long time. >> Let's go back to when you first like started the company. I know the biggest thing was like a Kickstarter campaign and I think you raised like $3 million through this Kickstarter campaign. How did that happen? Yeah. So, um we started the company and we had this idea to make the first uh to bring stereoliththography or resin 3D printing to the desktop. Um and also to make the first professional desktop printer because at the time there were starting to be a couple desktop printers, but they were really sort of hobbyists didn't work well. And we wanted to make something that would do what the $100,000 printers could do, but get it to everybody. We um built a prototype, borrowed some money from my cousin, built a prototype and then we raised a small round of venture capital and uh >> that was like 500k something like that. >> Uh 1.8 million was the [clears throat] first real round of funding and then about a year later which in retrospect which was pretty fast we launched the first product on Kickstarter the form one. And um >> what were you guys expecting going into that? We I I remember really well because it was, you know, it was a big moment and stressful and all that and we went out uh we went out for beers the you know the night before and we're kind of taking bets and um we knew we were going to beat the the target you put up on Kickstarter. That wasn't like that wasn't the main thing. But I think like the most optimistic that anyone else in the room was saying is like maybe we'll get to a million and I thought I think we can get to like one and a half million. And I everyone thought I was crazy and then we got to 3 million and um and like we got think we got to million in two days or something. So, it was really it was really fun in those first like the first day was just like non-stop celebrating basically cuz [laughter] like all our friends were coming by and like keeping following the Kickstarter and um it was really fun and then probably by the second day it was stressful again because now we knew we had to like ramp up our production and Yeah. and um and that was even more real than we expected. >> Yeah. Okay. So coming out of that and like you've got all these uh these orders now and you know hundreds and hundreds of people are kind of like counting on you. Uh how did you kind of think through actually getting those first printers out? >> We had a plan before that. It wasn't the right plan but uh >> what was that plan? Uh I think we had been before the Kickstarter had been planning to set up our own final assembly facility and like we had been considering contract manufacturer >> and after we saw the trajectory of the Kickstarter we knew we had to go with the contract manufacturer because it's going to be too much to ramp up. Um and you know I think we heard enough advice like it's harder to make things and ship them than you expect and like it's a 8020 kind of thing where it feels like you're done. you're not done. So, we like padded the timeline and all that, but of course, not enough. Um, so I think we were supposed to, but it wasn't too bad in the end. I think we announced it in September, said we would be delivering like in volume in February or March and we were doing it in May. So, >> so it was like actually relatively on schedule >> for a Kickstarter. Other Kickstarters, but it did not feel those few months did not feel good for sure. What was going through your mind while you know I I I know every great founder really hates like not making their customers happy and like having delays. So what was going through your mind at the time? >> I mean just every day's problem basically of like yeah we knew customers are waiting for us but it's not like you think about that all the time. you think about the next thing in front of you and um grinding through that and um and then we had another really uh I would say a 2x multiplier on the stress at that point because a few weeks after the Kickstarter we had been sued by the largest 3D printing company in the world for patent infringement. >> Hell yeah. Um, so we yeah, we got a I literally got a call out of the blue from the CEO of 3D Systems, um, saying, uh, that he's suing us for patent infringement. He started with a bunch of  about he didn't want to sue us and his board made him sue us, which is not not a good way to open a business relationship with >> I'm a great guy, but I got to do this. Sorry, dog. >> Yeah, [laughter] he really got got off on the wrong foot. >> Yeah. >> Um, but yeah, so we had that going on. um for the first few years of the company and like in the middle of this Kickstarter craziness and trying to fulfill it um we had this company trying to sue us out of existence and that was their explicit goal. They were their offer was like you can hand over your company and have jobs at 3D Systems. That was like the settlement proposal. So >> So I know uh you know now it may be kind of cool to get sued by a bigger company, but I imagine that in 2012 uh 2011 2012 it [snorts] wasn't as cool. Um, did you kind of think to yourself like this is the end at all during that time? >> I knew this was an existential threat and like pretty much any lawyer you talk to says like, "Oh yeah, the minimum you're going to spend fighting this is like three or four million or something like that." Like the total amount of money we had raised was less than 2 million. >> And then like all the orders combined >> Yeah. All the orders combined are three million. that's for the product we're going to deliver with like not amazing gross margins at that point. So, um >> if >> if it went the way most the lawyers told us it was going to go, we were not going to make it. Um, on the other hand, it didn't it was kind of fun to fight it because [laughter] well, like it actually helped that very early on they told us very clearly that like we want your company to go away and and we're kind of dicks so like you probably don't want to work with us. Uh, >> so you had like a little bit of a chip on your shoulder. >> Well, there was like no other option like I'm not >> I'm I'm not gonna >> roll over and die. >> Yeah, I'm not gonna roll over diamonds. I'm gonna try. Um, what was frustrating though, it just made everything else harder. It's not just dealing with that lawsuit, but every every single time you hire someone, they're like, "What's the deal with this lawsuit? Are you even going to be around?" Even customers are are asking and then um and then we did want to raise more venture capital and and that that was a huge huge uh multiplier to that that challenge. Um, but we managed to do all those things while the lawsuit was going on. And then eventually we paid them a bit of money but not that much and and that's all behind us. >> How did you navigate that? >> We mostly ignored what the lawyers were telling us. Um because everyone was kind of saying like you have to kind of settle it now because of all these negative impacts on the business >> and we said no actually we're going to like basically slow roll it uh while we because we know we're getting stronger quickly. Like we're we know people love this product and they're going to keep buying. were going to get revenue. Um, we also knew the patents were generally expiring in the next few years. So, we managed to um do various things to to kind of slow down the pace of of the lawsuit and um and then eventually when they saw there's not much time left on their patents, they you accepted a reasonable settlement. >> Interesting. Do you think uh it kind of like forged a specific type of culture because you had that happen so quickly? I think it definitely tested my abilities as a leader of getting people to focus on what's more what's important. Um is that at all points in a company um there's so many things you could be worrying about and u that that seem like the most important thing like what investors care about or this patent lawsuit or maybe in a later stage of the company the stock price. Um but most those things don't make any impact on like are you able to deliver more stuff to your customers and um and so this was an early strong test of like can we as a company and me you know personally help the team like stay focused on what really matters can I um try to shield the unnecessary stress uh from from people and just um help everyone like deliver an awesome product. >> Yeah. How much sleep were you getting at that time? >> I don't know. But that that was I only worked. I didn't didn't really have uh hobbies or family or anything else at that time. >> Do you think that was like the most stressful point or >> It was It was stressful. I I think the uh in general the my stress level is inversely related to how much money we're losing. So um uh so that was yeah definitely were burning money. I mean we weren't shipping anything either and the felt like the company was like it's like kind of we had almost just taken off but we were still like tethered to the ground and uh possibly about to crash. when you're kind of navigating that sort of situation where you have some fires that are needing to be put out, but you also have to keep on building the company like the lawyer fire is probably a big distraction. Uh, for the most part, how are you kind of thinking or making decisions dayto-day when you're in that environment? I think one of my strengths is kind of compartmentalizing the stress and like you take everything in, feel stressed, maybe talk with your uh you know friends or wife about it and get get the feelings out and then like and then you have to be able to step step back and say okay like what what do we need to do to to move forward? And then especially as um when you're in a company with a team, you have to think about what what can each person do and how can I help them do the best thing cuz you if I'm freaking out about something, if someone else can't really help me with that, like it's not not helpful. One of our one of our um board members has a nice line that in uh in a business stress should only flow in one direction and that's upwards. So like it while it's good to set like ambitious goals and push people to their to the limits of their ability like you don't need to bother them with something that that they can't do a lot about. That's just that's just unproductive stress. So better just >> it's it's good to not like you know just off the cuff let someone that's you know working on something know shit's hitting the fan and we might be >> Yeah. And it I mean it it was it was tough cuz we were um you one meeting Yeah. like you get off the phone with the CO3 systems, then you have to like go into a engineering design review and like not freak out and >> interesting. Yeah. How did you uh decide to kind of set the bar for the first like form one product that you wanted to create versus, you know, I I imagine you want to create somewhat of like a really great thing right off the bat, but I also understand that in order to do that, it takes years to really work out the kinks. >> Yeah. Um, I have a good answer for that because basically everything that was in the form two, which is a lot more than it's in the form one, >> was already like on the table when we were designing the form one. So like a cartridge system so you didn't have to pour resin in, Wi-Fi, touchscreen, a bunch of these things. Um, which we knew like should be in the final form of this product, but we cut them all for the form one because we knew they weren't actually critical to proving out this product. So, we did cut quite a bit from like what we would have liked to build and even then um it still wasn't we still didn't really have the resources to deliver what the form one should have been like it wasn't reliable enough. There was a lot of repairs and too high of an RMA rate and all that. Um, >> and did you know all this all the uh like maybe not necessarily issues but like slightly suboptimal things that you could have hypothetically made better on the next version that you had to ship the first version >> features that were missing I think we were pretty clear on yeah we dropped the Wi-Fi it's going to be okay. what proved to be much harder, which I think is true of like most products, but especially in 3D printing, is just making something reliable that executes even on its core function once you distribute it to a bunch of different people who use it in different ways and have manufacturing variation, all this stuff. It it can't be understated how hard that is. Like when your prototype that works in the lab meets the real world at scale, so many things break in so many different ways. And even though we like had enough people tell us that was be the case and we're trying to be um diligent about that like it's still worse than than you expect. >> What are the uh like decisions that if you could have gone back and kind of made different you know different calls? Uh what would those calls have been? >> The single biggest decision um that we should have made differently in the history of the company. We started with two ideas. One is to bring sterile lithography to the desktop. The other was to make a professional desktop printer. And most people think of that first one because that's like the obvious aspect of our product that we made sort of the first desktop resin printer. The second idea was actually more important and if we applied that idea to FDM, we would be a larger company today because ultimately that's a larger category than um than SLA or resin. >> So why did you decide to go after this instead of that? Um, at the time, um, I thought like completely incorrectly that we were like maybe too late for FDM because there were a couple of companies doing it. In retrospect, there's been like multiple generations of like failed companies since then. Like we could have tried multiple times since then and and the companies that were around then are completely irrelevant today like Makerbot. Um, so that was wrong and I think you know I I was doing masters at MIT and uh and I think I fell for the like probably the classic MIT startup failure mode of like well we need to work on we want to work on something valuable so like it must be something very hard and uh >> you got to feel the pain a little bit. >> Yeah. Well, that like if it's going to be like important new technology, it's going to be like really hard technology and like lasers and galvos and resin and all this stuff that like no one has made working on the desktop. Like that's that's really hard. Let's work on that. And FDM, this is this like glue gun on a three axis. Like that's not that hard. So like that's not that valuable. But that's that's really dumb. And and a lot of a lot of hard tech companies fail in that way where they like you you need to orient yourself around the problem, not the solution. This is such a basic mistake that so many startups, especially hard tech startups, make. They say like, "Oh, I figured out how to point lasers with a galvvinometer at photopolymers and that's really cool. Like, let me do something with that." Uh, but the world doesn't care about that. the world cares about what problem you solve for them and um and the more you focus on the problem and then be flexible about the solution and just say like well hell if I can solve that solution with an off-the-shelf thing great or uh yeah so you should orient around the problem not the solution >> I kind of love uh this is like the inverse of I think Warren Buffett talks about he wants to step over like one foot bars there's some people that want to do like the high jump hurdle over the 11 foot bar uh but there's sometimes things where the like output or value is not uh correlated with the difficulty of doing the thing. >> There's no correlation at all. And that's like that's like the basic thing that a lot of people get wrong is that's kind of reasonable to think that there might be but but there isn't. >> Yeah. Okay. What are you what are you doing today with that kind of like new framing in your mind? >> What are you doing today? Um I mean this has been years of evolution and [laughter] >> you're 14 years in. understanding. Yeah, I'm not sure I've still fully mastered uh that understanding. Um, >> what are we doing today? You always want to you [snorts] always want to look at like a lot of different things you could do and not just the first thing you get excited about. That's one thing that really helps because then when you can compare, it makes it a lot easier to say like, well, there's this really hard thing that might be valuable, but then there's this easy thing that's like equally valuable. So that makes it easier to pick. So trying to always like be considering a number of options and directions really really helps. Um and then I think thinking about the timeline that you want to contribute to the world in is really useful. So like there's really hard stuff that probably will be important 10 years from now. But for a product company like ours like the kind of right sweet spot is maybe like three year time frame. Like a full printer development is like maybe two and a half years something like that. So like thinking about what's what's the best thing we can get out there in about three years. Um and >> do you kind of keep that in mind while you're thinking about the like longer term arc of the business or how does that work? >> Yeah. Well, so I think we've gotten pretty good at this three-year thing and now we kind of need to add back in the five and 10 year view because there's also there are things that you can't do in in three years. Um, I just think that a lot of companies start with the 10-year thing and you won't survive 10 years like unless you do something good in in in three years. >> Do you think your story is a little bit more of being a cockroach? [laughter] >> Yes, I think um that's a reasonable description of uh of Form Labs. I mean, there are a lot of 3D printing companies that have raised a lot of money in the last 10 or 15 years, like billions of dollars. And I think we're like in the western world I think we're like the only successful large new 3D printing company in China. There's a number. I'm kind of thinking about this and this is I would almost consider it to be like uh Tesla where there's I don't know how many different auto companies have been started but none of them actually sur like even today many of the public companies are like negative gross margin to a drastic degree like Lucid and uh Rivian >> it's hard I mean that I think one thing that makes this business fun but also really hard is it's like unreasonably complex for what's not that big of a business we have to make. We have our engineering is like equal parts hardware, software and materials. We uh we manufacture hardware with contract manufacturers in China. We run our own chemical plant in Ohio. We sell in like a hundred different countries. We sell from like almost consumer level e-commerce up to like multi-million dollar enterprise sales. So there's like way too much going on and you kind of have to do all of it to deliver on the on that original vision of like I want to give you a 3D model and you give me a part. >> Well, we have to do all those things to to make that happen. >> So once you've built the form one and you're going towards the form two, how did you decide like what to build inhouse here versus like going to China and creating like that entire supply chain? >> So the form one was built with a contract manufacturer in San Diego. The form 2 was built with a contract manufacturer in Hungary. Um, and we were starting to leverage more and more Chinese suppliers. Um, >> how'd you find the chi uh Hungary supplier? >> So, >> project manufacturer. >> We wanted to go somewhere lower cost than the US and um my close partner here, David Lacto, our chief product officer, uh, his father happens to run the largest independent contract manufacturer in Hungary. So, um, so we had that connection. Uh um and we thought Hungary could be some kind of middle ground with between working with China, closer time zone and more English language and it worked. I mean form two was very successful. Um but I think you're asking like about what to do internally versus outsource. I think the right approach, I don't know if we've always done this, but like try to do as little as you can do to solve the problem and then like only take something on inhouse when uh when you're sure about two things. One is that like you really have a unique um problem. >> Mhm. >> Uh and two that you now know like enough about this problem that you're like approaching the state-of-the-art of knowledge. Um, and I think this is another place a lot of hard tech startups get it wrong. Like you need to try to do as little as you can do because just delivering whatever complete version you're trying to do is so hard. You like take on as little as possible and and we're a very vertically integrated company and we do a lot but it's like each one is like reluctantly basically. Um and and it gets it makes a lot more sense once you get a little bit bigger and you have the volume and you have the knowledge. It's a really good example from form two um the galvanometer. So the galvanometers are these special electric motors that position a mirror that we scan the laser in in a number of our products and um really cool because they position that mirror to like >> 100 nmter type accuracy at khertz. >> Um normally they're really expensive sort of specialized industrial components. We had found some lowcost ones that we use in a form one that are meant for laser light shows. Laser light shows don't really need to be accurate. So um so they're not exactly right. Um, >> it's a little bit it's a little bit like the uh SpaceX, you know, just using like an AC unit out of a refrigerator because it costs, you know, less than $1,000, whereas the rocket one costs a million or two. >> And my guess is like they probably don't actually use just an AC unit out of a refrigerator anymore, but they started with that, prototyped with it, and learned enough to now build a better one than any. So, we we went through that. So we as we're working with the Galvo companies and like helping them improve the accuracy, we're starting to realize like we kind of know how to build one of these and we're looking at the volumes we're approaching and like actually we might be the largest consumer of Galvos in the world at this point. So uh so we started like a parallel project to develop our own for the form 2 and um and we kept it like compatible so that if it failed we could use the the offtheshelf one. Um, and uh, it was like just at the end of the timeline when it like just started working and we swapped it in and that and that was a success. It makes the parts smoother, makes the the printer print faster and and we still build uh, design build our own galvos to this day. >> What do you think the biggest moments are throughout the company history where if things hadn't gone right, uh, bad things would have happened? They not have survived. Uh, I mean the obviously the the three systems patent lawsuit was a big big challenge. Uh, we've been sued two other times for patent infringement. Those weren't as existential. Uh, I mean the first like hardest hurdle was just the first round of funding. I was very hard for me. I'm not great at fundraising. If I didn't meet my co-founder Natan, who's much better with investors, I don't think we would have I don't think the company would have gotten started at all. >> Interesting. What kind of got you over that hurdle on that first fundraising round? >> Oh, it's a really good story. Um, we were like months into pitching different investors. We had like a prototype in our basement in Cambridge and um we'd like bring investors by and show them. It would only print for like a few minutes successfully at a time. So, we have to like time the pitch to uh to like bring them down to the basement while before it fails. Um, but people, especially in Boston, and this was 2011, in Boston, 2011, 23-year-olds with no work experience, starting a hardware company, nobody was funding that at all. There's like like approximately zero examples of that. So, but it was interesting enough that we'd meet with someone and they say, "I'm not going to invest, but like I know someone else who'd like to come and check out your basement. >> I'm not dumb, but like I know a guy." [laughter] >> Yeah. basically. Um, so we kept kept getting meetings and like months into that like we um we were at Legal Seafoods in Harvard Square, which is like a common place you'd have a a pitch dinner, and we were pitching VC from Best Buy, which apparently had a VCRM at that time. Um, at that point, we already learned the lesson that like if they're not interested like immediately, it's not going to work. So, we knew by the end of the dinner, there's no chance here. But then we got home. I shared an apartment with uh my other co-founder David Craner and we got he got an email from a friend of ours from MIT with like a screenshot of a retweet of an original tweet from M Caper uh saying on the patio at Legal Seafoods in Harvard Square overhearing two entrepreneurs pitching 3D printing. And we're like what the that's that's us. What the how did this even happen? How do we find this? And then we look up at M Caper and that's Mitch Caper who was the founder of Lotus Software, Lotus Notes, like basically developed the first killer app for the PC. Um, and we were amazed. If it was up to me, that was probably another point when we would have like failed to start the company and nothing would have happened. But luckily, David was more outgoing um than [snorts] me. But America is that place that they want their kids to grow up in. And the question is why, right? Why do you want your kid to grow up, right? And people in Europe live plenty nice lives. Um but I think it's what America represents that is particularly unique and there is no other country in the world and I think you know you look back say 500 years most people would bet against the United States right who are these people who are going to flee their countries move to a random spit of land that you know what the hell do they have and not just build a country build the most powerful country by a wide margin in nearly every industry the world has ever seen in a matter of a couple hundred years. Um and you know it it's it's up to us, right? It's up to the next generation to make that happen. It's not a not a it's not destiny unless we make it. Um, I think we have many incredible allies and we should actually value them more than we do in some some cases right now. And I I think also for me growing up I saw a pretty wide range of like political opinions and sometimes those political opinions swing so far into actually like disliking or hating America um for people who live in America. Um, which at some point like very early in my life, I think I I almost got like sucked down that rabbit hole of of, you know, bad things are happening here and so the country must be bad. And now looking back on that, I just don't understand how like if if someone and unfortunately I think there's a lot of people that fall in this bucket now, but if someone does not like America, please just leave. like you shouldn't be here. Um because the the opportunity to live here is so incredible. Um and there I I I think you know it is always worth pointing out the things that are going wrong and trying to fix them, but if you fundamentally don't believe that America is a great place, um I I you know, I think there's many other countries that you can you can go and live in. And so for for me like that that to me adds to kind of the the passion of how I feel about this because it's something I I saw and now like look look back on. You know though I think I think that idea exemplifies a lot of discourse about the difference between the American regime right our government and like our nation right um I don't know if we ought to especially as Americans especially in a nation where like freedom and autonomy and the individual and like our potential is so important I don't know if we necessar necessarily ought to feel as though our regime is beloved, right? Um, like why I care about America fundamentally, like there's there's two reasons, maybe more. I could probably list a bunch, but like one, it's it's it's because it's ours. Like it's because it's mine. It's because this is my home. Um, and I think that's a very it's perceived as a very anacronistic belief, you know, like to be in love with, to be loyal to the place that you were born in. um to to love where you are from, to love your soil. Um it's not to say that like there aren't many people that have come here and become Americans. Like my family is not uh like here from the 1600s or 1700s or something, but um like part of why I I love this place is because it's mine, right? Um and it's because it's ours. and then like what it what it means for it to be ours beyond again like the regime. This notion of like Republicans and Democrats uh I find pretty tiresome. Like I think that I won't put words in the mouth of all of you guys, but um like we're we're technologists, we're futurists, we care about what the world looks like 100 years from now and hope that it's better. Um, like part of why I love this country is because, you know, it's it's not just to to your point, I guess, Saurin, right? Like if you hate it here, um, like it's not just for anybody, like it's for those that aspire to greatness. And you need freedom and autonomy of the individual to allow for that, but like it's not just autonomy at large, right? Like it's not autonomy for the sake of uh, depravity. It's not autonomy for the sake of lethargy. Um, it's it's autonomy and freedom to be great, right? Like if you look at the the Puritans that set up uh the Massachusetts Bay Colony, like they came here not just because they were being persecuted and they were misfits, but because they wanted to set up utopia, right? Like if you look at the Cavaliers that came to the early American colonial South, like it's because they wanted to have great estates that they couldn't have in Europe because like they were out of space and because other people had a more hegemonic uh credentialist understanding of the social hierarchy and uh greatness unto itself was not valued. Um Teddy, you said like we were doing something new kind of, right? like kind of it was something new to be free as an individual, but in some sense it wasn't new at all. Like we based our nation's constitution and our government and our values off of Rome, right? Like I think that uh a lot of like nerdy autist guys on the internet can say like that they're really into Rome or something. Obviously, I think about it for a lot of particular and personal reasons, but like the United States, it's not the successor to Rome, per se, but spiritually like the notion of self-governance of the greatest people in the world for the sake of the well-being of the greatest people in the world. That is what excites me, right? It's not like a a White House and a picket fence. That's noble. That's fine. But it's because here in this nation and exclusively in this nation which is ours, we inspire to we inspire people to be great. Um like it's greatness. It's a more abundant future. It's a more glorious future. Like it's it's not perceived any longer as a virtuous thing to aspire for glory, right? Like people either consider that to be ego or consider it to be self-centered. Um, like if if if Gundo becomes what it ought to be, it it ought to be remarked as a glorious chapter in American history. Yes. One where people were free and where they thought about deep things and where they expressed themselves in unique ways. I feel in my heart, I know all of you do, I know you do too, Tai. I know that everybody in Elsagundo feels in their heart a desire not just for the freedom, but the freedom to be great among their staff and their friends and their loved ones and the rest of their compatriots. Like this country is worth defending and worth building up because it is great. Because we desire greatness.

QvDsgiuznRg.text: I'll also ask people when I'm hiring them like, "Are you prepared to go live on a mountain for six months?" Usually, it's something like, "Well, that sounds great. Sure, I can come back, see my family now, now and then." And it's like, "No, no, you can't. You have to go live on this mountain until the job is done." Since last I interviewed you in like March of last year, I think you were at like eight or nine employees. Uh, you've almost, you haven't quite done a 10x, but you're at like, you know, 6, seven, 8x that employee count. uh you went from working, you know, testing on farms to now you're doing, you know, full-blown operations in a few different countries and you're basically vert vertically integrating your entire business. Yes. Um so what's it been like, you know, growing through that? It's been pretty electric. Um trying to think of an easy way to to summarize all that activity. Um, well, I think that really the most salient explanation and starting place, right, is when Rain Maker deployed to Pendleton. Um, so some people know this, some people don't know this, uh, but we were originally testing in the Central Valley. Um, that's where we were flying our first drones. Raymaker at that time was essentially like a service uh, like a like a systems integrator and service provider. like we didn't build much. We didn't build drones. We didn't build radar. We didn't build our own way of dispersing the aerosol. We didn't make our own aerosols. Um we had very little software. We were a small team. Um but we were planning to just buy a bunch of these different pieces, mix them together, and then modify the weather that way. Uh none of that worked. Um regulatory. We uh were not permitted to continue testing in California. Let's put it that way. Um, and what was the time frame around there? That was September, October 2024. Yeah, it was really September 2024. So, up until that point in the company, we were learning a lot, but mostly just by getting punched in the face. Um, you know, like off-the-shelf hardware providers either were lying to us about their capabilities or their lead times were a year or they just didn't have like the the specifications that we even needed. Like there was nothing on market that we could get. Um, and then when we had to find permissible airspace to test in and more frequent conditions to test in, we surveyed the United States over trying to find an area where we could fly drones at altitude, sometimes at night, actually get a weather modification permit, all this kind of stuff. Um, and so we moved the entire company to Pendleton, Oregon, um, which is a little town on the border of Idaho. It's 16,000 people, which incidentally is about the same size as Elsa Gundo, but it is significantly less dense. 2,000 of the people that live there are prisoners. Uh most of the rest are farmers. And so we all moved into a warehouse and we found out pretty quickly that we had to vertically integrate everything. Um so from Oregon, from from October on, uh we decided to vertically integrate our drone. We decided to build our own radar. We decided to start doing our own particle design. Um that was harrowing. Uh right. Yeah. Like so you weren't planning on this. You just like had to do it. Yeah. Yeah. We had to. Uh and and you know like like I said, we needed more air. We needed more permissible airspace. We needed more frequent clouds to test in. Uh and then we found out we needed to vertically integrate all this stuff. Right. So you had your own version of the whole like Russia trip where you figure out, you know, everything costs way too much and it takes way too long. Yeah. Literally. Right. Yeah. No, we uh we we could not buy a rocket off the shelf. Um, and so those of us that lived through the Pendleton experience, I think that we all have like very different neurologies coming out of that. Um, because it was it was it was below zero most days. Uh, the warehouse that we were living in was ice cold. The heating barely worked. Uh, we converted all of the offices into bedrooms. Uh, that smelled awful by the end of it, candidly. Um, and that's really where like we we earned our stripes. Um, since then, since like October when we kicked it in gear and then December when we really started to get like our first evidence of effect from seeding, like from anthropogenic precipitation enhancement. Um, finally Rain Maker feels like the rocket ship that we always kind of knew it would. Um, but now, yeah, like we we are over 50 people. We're operating in Utah, Colorado, Oregon, California, Texas, a country in the Middle East that like a lot of people may know. Um, Argentina, we got our first guys deployed down there. Um, so it's uh it it is it is hypers scale time. Um, everywhere in the world should and will have weather modification program. I think every continent will have a weather modification program in about two years with Rain Maker. Um, we'll try to make that faster, but that's about the timeline that I'm thinking right now. So it feels it feels very fast finally which is good. Um you know a funny thing about starting early stage startups I'll stop rambling in a sec but it's like pre companies you're kind of just messing around like you're kind of just trying things you know um because you don't have enough money you don't have a big enough team especially in hardware to do much that's consequential. Uh so you just got to get to the point where like you have the capital and resources and personnel to like actually enact the vision. Um, and so it it feels like that is underway now. Like we're actively making water, which is very satisfying. Yeah. Um, so I know like a lot of different startups, you know, they put out uh basically some form of the Ernest Shackleton ad where it's like, you know, come work in terrible conditions and all this stuff and you'll love it. Uh, we'll pay you almost nothing, yada yada. Um, but I think it's kind of [ __ ] in most cases. In Rain Maker's case, it's actually very real. you know, if you want to live in a crate or like die on top of a, you know, snowy mountain, this is the place to be. How do you like filter for people like that? Yeah. So, you know, you're in part referencing like that Twitter post that I made, right? Um, so the rain maker forward operations core, it is, I think, tapping into this like latent spirit that all people have had since the beginning of time, but there's nowhere to express that and fulfill that in modern life. Um, and that's like the desire to adventure, the the desire to be like a real pioneer because, you know, you can you can go be a startup founder or employee in SF and like you're going to grind, right?
